> GObject is far from my area of expertise, but I do believe that the documentation is wrong for
UDisks.Filesystem.call_unmount_finish()
>
The docs state it takes 1 parameter, but it seems to require 2: the UDisks.Filesystem, and Gio.AsyncResult. 

It's a method of UDisks.Filesystem, so a UDisks.Filesystem gets always passed.

> Another probably related issue is that when I called it using the parameters passed to the unmount callback (FilesystemProxy and the result), it gave me a False result even though it did actually unmount.

pgi only supports the basics and is currently only used for creating the documenation.

I've seen this issue before, let me copy the relevant part from an [isso issue](https://github.com/posativ/isso/issues/206):

The compiler cannot find the so-called "C (C99) headers", which don't appear to be bundled with your version of VC++. This affects the compilation of any software which needs the C99 headers, including Python libraries which have C99 extensions such as Misaka.

You can still download free, open-source versions of the headers. Here are some relevant links:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/126279/c99-stdint-h-header-and-ms-visual-studio
http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/install_windows.html#visual-studio-and-cuda

The last link says:
> Finally download the stdint.h header from [here](http://msinttypes.googlecode.com/svn/trunk/stdint.h) and save it as C:\Program Files (x86)\Common Files\Microsoft\Visual C++ for Python\9.0\VC\include\stdint.h.

I apologize the rest of your comment was cutoff when I first read it. Let me give these things a try, thanks!

At least in that module, `fcntl` seems to be used purely for setting the pipes into non-blocking mode.

Here's a StackOverflow answer that explains how to do non-blocking pipe reads in Windows. (Apparently, Windows APIs use the term "overlapped" in place of "non-blocking")

http://stackoverflow.com/a/34504971/435253
https://gist.github.com/techtonik/48c2561f38f729a15b7b

Since it depends on the Win32 API, you'll want to follow the example of `os.path` and implement two different abstractions with the same API, then import one or the other depending on whether `os.name == 'nt'`.
This has been fixed in the meantime.

Hmm. From the traceback it looks like something is wrong with dnspython on your computer. It doesn't seem to be ntfy's fault. You could try reinstalling it?
If you're using python2: `sudo pip uninstall dnspython && pip install dnspython`
If you're using python3: `sudo pip3 uninstall dnspython3 && pip install dnspython3`

Looked at code.   The execution of code in aside() is relying on the fact that the `run()` function cancels all remaining tasks when it exits.  The `raise SystemExit()` in `_aside_term()` is forcing a kernel exit, which in term cancels everything else that's remaining.   It might make sense to perform a more orderly cancellation though.

Regarding handling of cancellation/exit, it is never really legal to write Curio code that ignores cancellation outright.  A task that catches a cancellation should either re-raise the exception or exit in some other way.

All output in Curio is routed through the logging module.   So, one way to silence the output would be to configure logging to ignore it.   There is also a debug flag to run that can silence it.

    curio.run(go, debug=None)

> I'm unaware that Curio promotes any threaded stuff for files, yet happily
> using aopen and friends in pure linear curio code. Can you provide some
> reference for such promotion?

I'm talking about:
https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/blob/db2aa37950e29d3763b10796c9234fcfa7c0d0cc/curio/file.py#L48
...which wraps all file methods (including `open()`) into `run_in_thread()`.
The code may be linear but a thread is still involved.
My proposal was to avoid that (`run_in_thread`) by default and leave that choice to the user which can either do this if the disk is fast enough:

```
await sock.sendfile(fd)  # blocking
```

...or this if it's not:

```
await run_in_thread(sock.sendfile, fd)  # non-blocking
```

> Native sendfile will block curio until it returns. fd -> Socket
> dumping will take time. So side stepping to a thread is needed.

My point is that modern disks should be fast enough so that the blocking time of a single `sendfile()` or `file.read()` call does not represent a problem for the main IO loop.

Sv is an operational unit for equivalent dose and effective dose. It is not a physical quantity
Gy is the physical quantity for absorbed dose.

The effective dose (in Sv) is calculated from the absorbed dose (Gy) after applying a set of factors which are quite subjective (furthermore, the definition of Sv is unclear for large doses, since the quality factors are not well defined in that case). This cannot be automated by a package such as pint. 

Pint deals with this [by defining Sv=Gy](https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/master/pint/default_en.txt), but since this is only true in some specific cases, **IMHO pint should define Gy and Sv as different (and, ideally, incompatible) units.**

For more details, see [this thread](https://www.researchgate.net/post/What_is_the_difference_between_Sievert_and_Gray_A_practical_question_concerning_the_SI_units_for_ionizing_radiation) and, specifically this post:

> Jerry J Battista
The University of Western Ontario
Allow me to clarify....
Absorbed Dose (Gray) is  purely Physical  as measured by radiation dosimeters (e.g. ion chamber, diode, TLD, film,  etc). The unit of  1 Gray = 1 J/kg - is simply the concentration of physical energy absorbed locally from the radiation exposure.
Effective Dose (Sv) wasintroduced to help incorporate the Biological impact of radiation but it still relies on the same fundamental dimensional unit (J/kg). It is the Physical Dose (in Gy) multiplied by unitless factors, such as Radiation Weights to account for different types (LET, RBE) of radiation, and/or Tissue/Organ Weights to account for differential radiosensitivity/importance of each organ to human survival. Dose rate corrections can also be applied. The origin of the organ weighting factors is poorly explained in ICRP documents. They are rough estimates, in my view, with unclear justification. 
Effective Dose (Sv) - The individual effective organ doses (in Sv) are summed together in a weighted fashion to yield a virtual whole-body Effective Dose. The Effective Dose is intended to match "overall risk" of a partial body non-uniform radiation exposure to a whole body uniform exposure.
Risk Estimation- The Effective Dose is often multiplied by risk-conversion factors derived from Japanese survivors with whole body exposures (e.g 5% per Sv of Effective Dose) to estimate the probability of a radiation -induced cancer death. Again  this is just an estimate for a similarly exposed population - not to be used for an individual exposed person.
In brief, dose (Gy)  is easy to measure physically...Effective Dose is much more complex, debatable,  and requires 'biological inputs' with very large uncertainties.
Radiation Protection is not (yet) at the accuracy level of Radiation Dosimetry !

As an example: assume you have data points of units metric_ton/hr and you integrate it over a period of 1 day with reference time-unit of seconds.
The ultimate unit would be metric_ton/hr*1day, which, when converted to base unit, spits out grams.

    from pint import UnitRegistry as ureg

    ureg = ureg()

    def revert_units(base,actual):
        comp = lambda x : ureg.Quantity.compatible_units(ureg(x))
        conv_array = []
        # derive conversion array
        for ubase in base.units.keys():
            for uactual in actual.units.keys():
                if bytes(comp(ubase))==bytes(comp(uactual)):
                    conv_array.append([ubase,actual.units[uactual]])
        # create new unit, compatible with the actual one on the base value basis
        new_unit = 1
        unit_gen = lambda x, y : ureg(x)**y
        for conv_item in conv_array:
            new_unit *= unit_gen(*conv_item)
        return actual.to(new_unit)

Example:

        base = 10*ureg('t/hr')
        actual = base*1*ureg('day')
        
        actual.to_base_units()
                = 240000000.0 gram
        revert_units(base,actual)
                = 240.0 metric_ton

Here is what I have found so far. For certain tables that lack a missing value in the last cell of the last row and that are followed by an internal HTML hyperlink (and possibly other text too), the tool will chew up the last cell and cause errors in the final HTML docs generation. On further debugging, this seems to happen because of a potential bug in the mistune library where, in `parse_table()`, the value of the matched group (`m.group(3)` if you are looking at the code) for cells includes a spurious newline at the end of the text that is being parsed. This is likely happening because of the rule matching in `parse()` against the rule table (see `manipulate` in mistune):

```
table = re.compile(
        r'^ *\|(.+)\n *\|( *[-:]+[-| :]*)\n((?: *\|.*(?:\n|$))*)\n*'
    )
```

It is possible to show that this bug goes away if I `rstrip()` the matched group before breaking it up into cells inside mistune's `parse_table()` but I am still not sure what the right fix is in this case or what might be wrong in the above regex.

Here is the code I'm using to compile the custom MIBs (much of the code was mirrored from mibdump.py)

```
def compile_custom_mibs(mibcompiler, mibpath, compiled_dir):
    if os.path.exists(compiled_dir):
        shutil.rmtree(compiled_dir, ignore_errors=True)

    os.mkdir(compiled_dir)

    mibSearchers = PySnmpCodeGen.defaultMibPackages
    mibStubs = [x for x in PySnmpCodeGen.baseMibs if x not in PySnmpCodeGen.fakeMibs]
    searchers = [PyFileSearcher(compiled_dir)]

    for mibSearcher in mibSearchers:
        searchers.append(PyPackageSearcher(mibSearcher))

    searchers.append(StubSearcher(*mibStubs))

    custom = ":".join(["file", mibpath])
    mibcompiler.addSources(*getReadersFromUrls(*[custom], **dict(fuzzyMatching=True)))

    precompiled_path = os.path.dirname(mibs.__file__)
    precompiled_reader = FileReader(precompiled_path)
    precompiled_reader.exts = ['.py']
    borrower = PyFileBorrower(precompiled_reader, genTexts=False)

    mibcompiler.addBorrowers(borrower)
    mibcompiler.addSearchers(*searchers)

    mibnames = []
    for filename in os.listdir(mibpath):
        mibnames.append(os.path.splitext(filename)[0])

    mibcompiler.compile(*mibnames)
```

The `precompiled_path` is the directory that the .py/.pyc files that come packaged with pysnmp are located.

Hi @rasbt,

thanks for the quick response.

The algorithm indeed looks like StackingCVClassifier, but adapted for regression. It looks like there are some subtle differences though.

I'm fine with your naming suggestion, only remark is: There is no cross-validation going on in the algorithm as far as i can see?  I don't have a strong opinion on this naming though.  Happy to go with your choice.

The algorithm divides training data into K folds. I then trains N instances of each base model type, each on K-1 parts of training data. Each instance makes predictions of the remaining piece of training data. Then the predictions for each model are concatenated and used as input for the second level model. This is all identical to the StackingCVClassifier.

Now the difference starts, the N instances of the base model are kept, and used during predicting. There it does N predictions, one with each instance of the base model, and averages them as input for the second level model. Then the second level model predicts final output. 

I'm not a theoretical ML expert, so i'm not sure what approach works better.  Perhaps the ideas from StackingCVClassifier would even be an improvement?  I would be happy to run some experiments and then code the best performing version.

Best regards, Eike

Hmm, currently because of global string constant every binary linked with dlib will have its size increased by 157kb.
This can be easily fixed by getting rid of static std::string variable (done).
Was:
- dynamic and static dlib libraries are larger by 157kb
- binaries linked statically/dynamically with dlib will be larger by 157kb and will always have 157kb of `obj_detector_dat` copy in `obj_detector_s` at runtime
- call to `get_serialized_frontal_faces` costs nothing because it returns reference to static std::string

Now:
- dynamic and static dlib libraries are larger by 157kb
- object linked with static library will only have serialized face detector data included when `get_serialized_frontal_faces` is called from application (ie only when they use dlib's frontal face detector). No long-living extra copies of detector data are made
- each call to `get_serialized_frontal_faces` makes temporary copy of it.

You shouldn't wrap the logger up into another function. `get_logger()` is meant to be called only once with [the same `name`](https://github.com/reubano/pygogo/blob/910fa6ef73a172c4b02c1c9e942ba9f57d30914e/pygogo/__init__.py#L295) (or in this case no `name`). For every extra instance of the same logger you create, each time you log it will make a duplicate message. You should do something like this instead:

```python
file = utils.open_file_from_project_root('resources\\log_file.json') 
log_json = json.loads(file) 
log_path = os.path.join(log_json['path'], log_json['filename']) 

logger = gogo.Gogo(
    'json', 
    low_hdlr=gogo.handlers.file_hdlr(log_path), 
    low_formatter=gogo.formatters.json_formatter, 
    high_level='error', 
    high_formatter=gogo.formatters.json_formatter).logger
```
Then just import `logger` and [use it where you need it](https://github.com/reubano/pygogo#id1). If you really do need multiple loggers, [see this example](https://github.com/reubano/pygogo#id4).

Whoa, that's not expected. I was getting [{'a': 1, 'b': 2}, {'a': 1, 'b': 2}] in my test (updated + appended). That's probably not the behavior you want either. May need to take a step back and write some example cases.

So the one we just solved:

```
base = {'zones': [{'a': 1}]}

overlay1 = {'zones': [{'a': 1}]}

>>> remerge([base, overlay1]) 
{'zones': [{'a': 1}]} 
```

And now the one we need to solve:


```
overlay2 = {'zones': [{'b': 2}]}

>>> remerge([base, overlay2]) 
{'zones': [{'a': 1}, {'b': 2}]}  # desired
```

See I think these behaviors are mutually exclusive. One updated in-place, and the other appended. If we were to make it behave like the latter, then the result would become `[{'a': 1}, {'a': 1}]`. While the `{'a': 1}` dictionaries may look the same to us, remap/remerge has no way of knowing that. So, what to do?

Built-in (as in written in C, not necessarily part of the standard library) objects and modules (being objects themselves) are indeed prime examples of entry points.  Built-in functions, well, they are interesting beasts. 

I thought of entry points as objects available in the analyzed module, but created/modified outside of Jedi's reach, in a space you can think of as "terra incognita" and the entry points would be objects that "enter" the analyzed module from that "terra incognita". So a built-in function most likely being a part of a built-in module or a method on a built-in object, can be an entry point, too. What's interesting is that the function is also a short instantiation of that "terra incognita". That means, the returned values become entry points, and so do after-exit values of mutable function arguments. In rare cases, those built-in functions might also alter module-level objects.

Now that I have written this down, I wonder if tracking separate entry points is actually a good idea...

I think you have to change the underlying class with which requests are made. By default, I use `PoolManager`. To go through a proxy, you need `ProxyManager`.

```python
import urllib3
import telepot.api

proxy_url = 'http://localhost:3128/'

telepot.api._pools = {
    'default': urllib3.ProxyManager(proxy_url=proxy_url, num_pools=3, maxsize=10, retries=False, timeout=30),
}

telepot.api._onetime_pool_spec = (urllib3.ProxyManager, dict(proxy_url=proxy_url, num_pools=1, maxsize=1, retries=False, timeout=30))
```

You may also need to put in a custom header. Read [urllib3 docs](http://urllib3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/managers.html#proxymanager) for details.

import logging
import time
from datetime import datetime
from kafka import KafkaProducer

KAFKA_SERVERS = ["node-1:9092", "node-2:9092", "node-3:9092"]
KAFKA_TOPIC = "debug1"
SLEEP = 30

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG, format="%(asctime)s %(name)-12s %(levelname)-8s %(message)s")

producer = KafkaProducer(bootstrap_servers=KAFKA_SERVERS, retries=0)

for i, msg in enumerate(["msg1", "msg2"]):
    try:
        print "step %d, sleep %d seconds" % (i, SLEEP)
        time.sleep(SLEEP)
        """
        step 0:
            iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 9092 -j REJECT
            iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --dport 9092 -j REJECT
        step 1:
            iptables -F
        """
        dt = datetime.now().strftime("%Y%m%d%H%M%S")
        producer.send(KAFKA_TOPIC, bytes(msg + "_" + dt)).get(timeout=1)
        print "sent: %s" % msg
    except Exception as ex:
        print ex

producer.close()
```

Ah, OK. I see the problem. You haven't set `prefix` so WhiteNoise is serving your static files at the URL root. If you request http://localhost:8000/style.css you'll see the file being served by WhiteNoise.

The fix is just to set `prefix` as well as `root`:
```python
app.wsgi_app = WhiteNoise(app.wsgi_app, root='static/', prefix='static/')
```

But I'd also be tempted to make `root` an absolute path, rather than relying on the app being run with a particular working directory:
```python
import os

app.wsgi_app = WhiteNoise(app.wsgi_app,
        root=os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), 'static'),
        prefix='static/')
```

@antonio-osorio seems like it is possible to clean lock collection even without autoremove option. You can use mongo TTL collections, in that case you can just pass collection=my_lock_collection_with_ttl to MongoLock constructor. 
For example:
```
from pymongo import MongoClient
from mongolock import MongoLock

my_lock_collection = MongoClient().my_db.my_lock_collection
my_lock_collection.create_index('created', expireAfterSeconds=3600*24)

with MongoLock(collection=my_lock_collection) as lock:
    # do some staff
```

But if this solution is not suitable for you - we can introduce`autoremove=True` option to `lock` and `__call__` methods signature. Please let me know if it is the case.

Enabling multiple default files in the `add_argument` method makes it more consistent with the constructor-based approach. I don't see any other way to allow specifying multiple default values (of which only the one highest priority is actually used) while enforcing the command-line argument to accept only a single value. If the behaviour is properly documented, I don't see a problem with this approach.
The purpose of this behaviour is to allow writing e.g. `default=["/etc/foo/foo.conf", "~/.config/foo/foo.conf"]` and make e.g. `--config bar.conf` valid, but `--config bar.conf --config baz.conf` invalid. IMHO this is a very common behaviour situation. Also note that as per the second point above, only one of the multiple supplied configs should be read.

I suspect the problem here is you aren't giving the API method a `Length` object. `Length` is the superclass for the classes like `Inches`, `Cm`, `Pt`, etc. in `pptx.util`.

It's possible that if you do the math in the other order that it would work, like `prs.slide_width * 0.1` instead of `0.1 * prs.slide_width`, but I'd have to check the code to see if `Length` objects have a `__mul__()` method.

Anyway, if you surround the arithmetic with an `Emu()` constructor that should work either way.

```python
from pptx.util import Emu
left = Emu(0.1 * prs.slide_width)
```

There's some more information in the documentation about English Metric Units (EMU), which is an integer format (914400 EMU to the inch), which might help you make sense of the behavior. I believe the API states that the position and size parameters need to be `Length` objects, which take care of all the integer conversion properly at the right moment in the code.

This appears to be somewhat possible on _win32_, _X_ and _OSX_.

 *  On _win32_, [GetCursorInfo](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms648389(v=vs.85).aspx) combined with `LoadCursor` could possibly be used.

From the Apache/ActiveMQ web page:

Working with JMS Text/Bytes Messages and Stomp
Stomp is a very simple protocol - that's part of the beauty of it! As such, it does not have knowledge of JMS messages such as TextMessages or BytesMessages. The protocol does however support a content-length header. To provide more robust interaction between Stomp and JMS clients, ActiveMQ keys off of the inclusion of this header to determine what message type to create when sending from Stomp to JMS. The logic is simple:

| Inclusion of content-length header | Resulting Message |
| yes | BytesMessage |
| no | TextMessage |


This same logic can be followed when going from JMS to Stomp, as well. A Stomp client could be written to key off of the inclusion of the content-length header to determine what type of message structure to provide to the user.

## RLE (run-length encoding)

This is simple compression method, but worst for distance analyse.

```python
>>> from textdistance.algorithms.compression_based import NCD, rle_ncd
>>> NCD()._rle('qwe')
'qwe'
>>> NCD()._rle('qwee')
'qwee'
>>> NCD()._rle('qweee')
'qw3e'
>>> NCD()._rle('qweeee')
'qw4e'
>>> NCD()._rle('qweqwe')
'qweqwe'
```

# NCD

`NCD(A, B) = [len(Z(A + B)) - min(len(Z(A)), len(Z(B)))] / max(len(Z(A)), len(Z(B)))`

```python
>>> len(NCD()._rle('qweqwe'))
6
>>> len(NCD()._rle('qwe'))
3
>>> assert rle_ncd('qwe', 'qwe') == (6 - 3) / 3 == 1
```

```python
>>> len(NCD()._rle('t' * 3))
2
>>> len(NCD()._rle('t' * 6))
2
>>> assert rle_ncd('ttt', 'ttt') == (2 - 2) / 2 == 0
```

NCD by RLE only indicate prefix and postfix equality:

```python
>>> NCD()._rle('attt')
'a3t'
>>> NCD()._rle('ttta')
'3ta'
>>> NCD()._rle('attt' + 'ttta')
'a6ta'
>>> assert rle_ncd('attt', 'ttta') == (4. - 3) / 3 == 1. / 3
```

So I envision non-default servers only really being useful for applications *other* than `wormhole send` (i.e. apps which `import wormhole` and use a different APPID, probably apps which are written by somebody who's maintaining their own wormhole server, so they bake in their own URLs instead of using my default). 

As a result, I'm not sure whether I'd recommend packaging systemd/init.d/etc scripts. (I'd certainly not recommend packaging scripts which launch a server with the default APPID, since there's only supposed to be one server for that one).

This is a negligence of requests, not furl. Requests should not explicitly check
that `params` and `data` subclass collections.Mapping. Instead, it should check
for the existance of an `item()` method and invoke such directly. Duck typing.

That said, it's important for furl to interoperate with as many libraries as
possible, and there's no downside for omdict to subclass dict directly.

The next version of orderedmultidict, v0.7.7, will subclass dict and allow for
both of your desired requests use cases:

```python
>>> import requests
>>> from orderedmultidict import omdict
>>> o = omdict([('a', 'a'), ('b', 'b')])
>>> requests.get('http://httpbin.org/get', params=o).json().get('args')
{u'a': u'a', u'b': u'b'}
```

```python
```python
>>> import requests
>>> from orderedmultidict import omdict
>>> o = omdict([('a', 'a'), ('b', 'b')])
>>> requests.post('http://httpbin.org/post', data=o).json().get('data')
u'a=a&b=b'
```

I'll update this issue when orderedmultidict v0.7.7 is hot off the anvil.

This confirms my suspicion. The server returned an HTTP 503 status code on the first attachment request, but added the attachment anyway. We interpreted the HTTP 503 as a request to try again later, which is why the attachment is added multiple times.

I'm not sure what the best solution is here. The server provided zero context with the HTTP 503, so we can't distinguish it from at real error where we want to retry. You could try to change from using a `ServiceAccount` instance to a `Credentials` instance. You can then catch this error manually and only retry after fetching a fresh item and determining if the attachment was indeed added.

I see the rational. However, in the case of a db query where the number of objects in the queryset exceed 999, SQLite will break. Even on other db backends, huge IN clauses come with undesirable performance implications.

In addition, the memory usage will start to go high, so an iterator is generally what you want to do. Better to have something go slower, than to simply stop working above a certain size.

You can implement the two-query approach very easily on the result of the iterator, in any case, if it suits your purpose.



> On 21 Jul 2015, at 19:08, Lorenzo Peña <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
> In my opinion, it's always a good idea to return querysets, so that further filtering may be applied at the user discretion.
> 
> As for using queryset.iterator, it only makes sense for intermediate queries like the one collecting the duplicates. Django docs say that iterator won't work well with prefetch_related because they cancel each other (iterator prevents caching while prefetch_related cache results). Therefore, it makes no sense to prevent further caching (or deactivate previous anti-caching behaviour) on querysets being returned by your methods.
> 
> This is mainly motivated by an actual example in a project I'm working on: I changed the code for installed django-reversion. Am currently using a select_related on revision and revision__user, and a prefetch_related on object, it drastically reduces db overload when rendering a view with many (many!) versions that shows details about the user and the related object.

In general the approach of this parser is to trust the input. If someone says their name is `Big.Kah00na!` we simply take those characters and put it into the right bucket depending on how/where it appears in the string. The only characters the parser cares about are ones that indicate various name parts, so far they are `,&()` and, of course, whitespace. There are only 3 supported variations of comma placement, so I think `,.Mick (mickjagger) Jagger` is not a case this parser needs to support. 

So I think the most the parser could do to clean up the input is provide some hooks for you to do things at various stages of parsing. Currently you could subclass `HumanName` but I agree that could be intimidating. If we want to provide easier tweaking I prefer the idea of passing in functions that could be used to do more than just remove characters. (maybe removing characters could be the example in the docs.) But I'm not sure what advantage that provides over just doing it outside of the parser, e.g. `HumanName("Mick (@mickjagger) Jagger".replace("@",""))`?

Removing a period at the beginning of name parts would be one place where a post-parsing hook would be handy, then you could look at each parsed name piece and strip off any leading periods, but you'd need to know how the parser works and the internal data structure. But automatically handling misplaced periods is tricky and beyond the scope of this library, there's too much variation in the mistake to write rules about how to correct it.

What is your dataset? What are you trying to do exactly with the parser? Which of the examples you provided are realistic examples from the input you are dealing with?

It is JSONPath spec that these are the same, as far as JSON goes.

But in fact, preliminary versions of this library supported object fields as well as dictionary entries, since there is no rule that JSON has to be deserialized into dicts and lists, etc. The trouble is that there are ambiguous cases where it is not clearly right to do one or the other.

I am open to ideas. A couple that come to mind:

 - Global configuration as to whether you want to query into objects or dicts+lists
 - Add a new backtick operator like \`field\` (or even aliased to `f`) that either gets one field, or switches behavior for a whole sub-JSONpath expression.

Yes it is, all public methods of a data model are available dynamically, `search` is just one of them, just pass the keyword arguments:

``` python
>>> import odoorpc
>>> odoo = odoorpc.ODOO('x.x.x.x')
>>> odoo.login('db', 'admin', 'password')
>>> odoo.env['res.partner'].search([])
[3, 23, 22, 28, 27, 29, 25, 33, 35, 32, 21, 6, 8, 9, 7, 1, 5, 34]
>>> odoo.env['res.partner'].search([], limit=4)
[3, 23, 22, 28]
>>> odoo.env['res.partner'].search([], limit=4, offset=2)
[22, 28, 27, 29]
```

Then pass the result to the `browse` function:

``` python
>>> Partner = odoo.env['res.partner']
>>> ids = Partner.search([], limit=4, offset=2)
>>> for partner in Partner.browse(ids):
...     print partner.name
...
```

This implementation of the desired functionality feels fairly heavy to me, for a couple of reasons:

1. Having to add a new subclass to every `Page` model introduces new migrations everywhere and requires overriding `serve` and `route` for each page type (although, I suppose, creating another mixin could handle that). `serve` and `route` in particular are pretty important functions and it'd be nice not to have to rely on users of this package overriding them unless absolutely necessary.
2. The separate UI for flag definition means that viewers of the Wagtail admin "flags" section won't easily be able to see if or how many pages rely on a particular flag. From a user permissions point of view, this also gives people with only edit permission the ability to modify flag behavior.

More generally, this behavior expands the scope of this package significantly, from just defining/checking flag states to actually defining what behavior happens when a flag is set. There's already `flagged_url`, but the use case for that helper seems more clear cut and necessary than this one.

Could this same functionality be achieved with built-in Wagtail hooks like this:

```py
# wagtail_hooks.py
from wagtail.wagtailcore import hooks

@hooks.register('before_serve_page')
def serve_some_pages_as_draft(page, request, args, kwargs):
    if flag_enabled('MY_FLAG', request=request):
        page = page.get_latest_revision_as_page()
        return page.serve(request, *args, **kwargs)
```

Then you'd define the flag using built-in wagtail-flags behavior, either through the UI for dynamic control or in settings like

```py
# settings.py
FLAGS = {
    'MY_FLAG': {'path': '/path/to/page/'},
}
```

This could also (somewhat hackily) support flagging of multiple pages or page patterns by creating a more complex regular expression, e.g. `/page1/|/page2`). Future support for OR and AND conditions in wagtail-flags would make this easier.

Hi @TomAugspurger A high value data set for you to try out here is the one used in this Phase I notebook:

 * https://github.com/ContinuumIO/elm/blob/master/examples/temperature-PDFs-clustering.ipynb

It is, if I recall correctly, the same MERRA2 surface temperature data set used by:
```
Classifying reanalysis surface temperature probability density functions (PDFs) over North America with cluster analysis
P. C. Loikith, B. R. Lintner, J. Kim, H. Lee, J. D. Neelin, and D. E. Waliser

GEOPHYSICAL RESEARCH LETTERS, VOL. 40, 3710–3714, doi:10.1002/grl.50688, 2013
```
(I'll send you that paper privately for copyright reasons).  

It's a bit of set up because it is 1000+ NetCDFs that must be downloaded, but would make a good demo for promotion of these tools.  The idea of the paper is taking 1000+ surface temperature 2D rasters (one NetCDF each), concatenating them in time to 3D, histogram the temperature deviations along the time dimension into 150 bins, and use the log counts within those histogram bins as input features to clustering.  They're using the PDF of each raster cell's temperature time series as a way of classifying climates.

logger doesn't require restart when the drain url changes.  logspout does require restart if the logger's IP changes-- which can happen if it is ever rescheduled to a different host for some reason.  This will be addressed if we ever get around to upgrading logspout (waiting for their upcoming stable release has been a blocker).  Also, we've periodically seen logspout seize up (deadlock), and to our knowledge, we've addressed those issues, but I'd not be surprised if this were still somehow possible.  If this happens to you again, checking logspout's logs (on each host) and logger's logs may be enlightening as to what's going on.  Using `deis logs` to look for recent app logs should also give you some indication of whether logs are reaching the logger.  If not, that is more indicative of a logspout problem.

_I don't understand the working of Pipenv in anywhere near the detail that others on this thread do, and don't want to take it off-topic. If the below is useful, great. If it's not, sorry!_

We use some nasty hax:
- To find the version to update to, we currently just get the latest version (we should use the resolver in `pip-tool`, but don't yet. Turns out that's not so bad as the next step will error if it's unresolvable)
- To produce a `Pipfile.lock` that only updates that (top-level) dependency, we
  1. Update the Pipfile entry for the dependency we're updating to support the latest version
  2. Parse the existing lockfile
  3. Update the Pipfile to set the requirement on every other dependency to what it resolved to in the lockfile
  4. Run `pipenv lock` to generate a new lockfile
  5. Update the hash in the newly generated lockfile to be what it would be if we hadn't done step 3 (a horrible hack that I'd really like to be able to remove)

As I understand it, `pipenv lock --keep-outdated` would allow me to use the following flow instead:
  1. Update the requirement on the dependency we're updating to **equal** the latest version
  2. Run `pipenv lock --keep-outdated` to generate a new lockfile
  3. Update the hash in the newly generated lockfile to be what it would be if we had only updated the Pipfile to **support** the latest version

So, that would be a bunch better. Ideal would be to be able to run `pipenv lock --keep-outdated <some_package_name>`, but beggars can't be choosers! :octocat:
I don't have an opinion on ``.lock`` vs ``.freeze``, so I'm fine with either.

error - Error messages that typically result in a non-zero exit code.
warning - Messages to be shown to users that are useful in an interactive scenario.
info - Standard log messages. Will be shown to user if `--verbose` specified.
debug - Debug log messages. Will be shown to user if `--debug` specified.

@mingfeiy Hello, I have mailed you but haven't heard back. It would be great if you could reply as I need to get going fast.

Yeah, I've been lucky enough to be warned early on to never use [] and {} as default parameter values, so I never experienced that pain :)

There's a difference between u'' and None when it comes to *serializing* to xml.  None means the xml element won't appear at all, u'' means it will appear and be empty.  As far as *deserializing*, there's no difference.

It may be that None is more convenient for the user (opt in), otherwise you have to explicitly set the values you don't want to None, which isn't as obvious.

Changing that default from u'' to None has potential to break existing code though.  I would prefer you don't change any of them from what they used to be.  For the new one you are introducing, you can make that one None, in fact I think you have to, to avoid breaking existing code that doesn't know about that new field.

I think there is some confusion here. The problem concerns the events that have several impacted records.
For example, a batch request that «puts» 3 records on the same collection. You'll see that `record_id` and `uri` in the event payload don't make sense. I suggest that we remove `${resource_name}_id` and leave `uri` with the value of the plural endpoint when there is more than one impacted record.

I think we need to bake the concept of "dev" releases into the versioning scheme. something like:

x.x.y-dev

marks a version as a development version.

then you need to explicitly tell conda you want dev versions, either with a flag to the install command (allowing any dev version) or adding the -dev to the version for a particular package.

So conda forge would likely end up with something like:

my_package 1.2.3
my_package 1.2.4
my_package 1.2.5
my_package 1.3.0-dev

at some point, and no one would get the dev version unless they asked for it.

then, later on, when released:
my_package 1.2.3
my_package 1.2.4
my_package 1.2.5
my_package 1.3.0-dev
my_package 1.3.0

(the -dev version could be removed later on)

of course, there could be multiple dev versions -- but I kind of like the idea of there only being one -- when a new build gets put up, it replaces the old one. not at all reproducible, but as long as teh package itself has a checksum or git tag, or something to identify it, then that's OK.

Alternatively, we could add VCS label on the end:

my_package 1.3.0-dev-62ac5ccb32c0361e9

though I can't think at the moment how conda could know _which_ -dev version is the latest.

maybe use a tag, rather than a hash, so it's sort-able? that would require putting a tag in when a dev release was done, but that makes sense anyway.

-CHB

The `~/.conda` directory is no longer used on Windows, only unix.  Conda's install location is not guaranteed to be user-writable.  Conda always needs at least one user-writable location for the package cache (`pkgs` directory) and the `envs` directory where new named environments are created.  The default location for these directories is `~/.conda` on unix, and on Windows we use [appdirs](https://github.com/ActiveState/appdirs).  The `~/.conda` directory has been used for years on unix for this purpose.

When conda's install location is user-writable, then that's the location chosen as "first writable" for these two directories.  When conda's install location is not writable by the current user, then conda uses the `~/.conda` directory as the writable location, but is still able to use the install location for a read-only package cache and named environments.  Compare output of `conda config --show` for the two different cases.

The locations of these directories can be overrode by configuration using the `envs_dirs` and `pkgs_dirs` configuration parameters, or `CONDA_ENVS_DIRS` and `CONDA_PKGS_DIRS` environment variables.

Given that the `~/.conda` directory has been used for this purpose for multiple years, moving it would be a major change that would require discussion and general community buy-in.  Moving it could end up confusing people or pissing them off if conda leaves GB worth of old packages around.

Speaking of conda-smithy, you should be aware of the 4.3.0 feature release change log.
https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.3.0/CHANGELOG.md

Specifically

> * **Python API Module**: An oft requested feature is the ability to use conda as a python library,
  obviating the need to "shell out" to another python process. Conda 4.3 includes a
  `conda.cli.python_api` module that facilitates this use case. While we maintain the user-facing
  command-line interface, conda commands can be executed in-process. There is also a
  `conda.exports` module to facilitate longer-term usage of conda as a library across conda
  conda releases.  However, conda's python code *is* considered internal and private, subject
  to change at any time across releases. At the moment, conda will not install itself into
  environments other than its original install environment. (#4028)

@kalefranz @goanpeca it would be great if you can clarify how you plan to update the conda installer. We are preparing material to be printed and to be used by people without experience with conda. We currently instruct to answer `yes` to the question if the path should be augmented during installation and use `source activate` instructions later. We wan't to make sure that these instructions don't fall out of date quickly after print.

In particular the concerns are:
- If the question changes and the `conda` shell function is defined by default, our `source activate` 
instructions may break.
- We can instruct users to say `no` to the PATH augmentation and manually add the shell function definition, but this adds additional complexity and we are not sure why it is not done by default instead (ie. may there lurk some other issues with this)?

The UCS problem is the following.  Python 2 has a configuration option `--enable-unicode` which may be set equal to `ucs2` or `ucs4`, which sets the number of bytes used to represent unicode characters in memory.  The problem is that C extensions compiled against a Python using one setting will generally not work with a Python which was using the other one.  On Mac and Windows, `ucs2` is used by the binary installer from python.org, which is why we use `ucs2` on Mac and Windows in Anaconda as well, such that binary packages from other sources play well with Anaconda.  Now for Linux, there is no de-facto standard.  Some Linux distros use `ucs2`, and some `ucs4`.  We've decided early on to use `ucs4` on Linux.  Now if someone pip installs conda on a Linux system which uses `ucs2`, then our Python packages (the ones with C extensions) will not work with their system Python.

I don't think it is feasible to have a shared package cache that any user can update.  It's a security nightmare, since you can specify arbitrary channels and any user can force it to pull trojaned version of a package from malicious repo.  Signed and fully namespaced packages would probably help with that.  

> any thoughts on how to deal with a conda user that should be able to write to a user's home directory on debian or redhat?

I think when a user runs `conda` it should run as their user and do what it needs to do.  The package cache sync/pull is a separate issue.  The create or update conda environments step could still be run as the current user.  

I guess a caching web proxy would be similar to the functionality.

I think that the proper resolution here for the _trailing_ wildcard is to _eliminate the use of regexes_ and rely on `VersionOrder` instead. So, for instance, `2.7*` matches any normalized version with 2 and 7 in the first two "slots". In this case, it's equivalent to `>=2.7,<2.8`, but we can't always translate to numerical inequalities, because we might also want to handle cases like this: `2.7post*`. So it really is a special kind of match.

I'm unaware of any actual use of the wildcard in a _non-trailing_ position. In my view that should be deprecated altogether.

I think it's a bit safer to pin `oslo.config>=1.12.0` as I did in #183. New versions of `pbr` are fine, it's just that `oslo.config` mistakenly had `pbr` as a runtime requirement at all.

There was a new release of [python-keystoneclient](https://github.com/openstack/python-keystoneclient/releases) yesterday, but it still has `oslo.config>=1.11.0`, which can create an incompatible version problem for `pbr`. So really this needs to be fixed in python-keystoneclient, but barring that I'd argue updating `oslo.config` is a safer fix.

Hi @zxsimple, I'm basically trying to say that benchmarking a small program on a single machine against a distributed version of the same small program will almost always show that the single machine is faster.  An exaggerated example of this would be something like printing the numbers 1 to 1000.  If I run this on a single machine, it will probably finish in microseconds to milliseconds.  But if I write a distributed Spark program to print these numbers using 10 executors, you will incur all the overhead of starting Spark on distributed nodes along w/ various costs of communicating RDDs over the network.

So to re-iterate, the distributed mnist example code was not provided to demonstrate any performance gains, but rather to demonstrate the mechanics of distributing a simple program (e.g. like "printing 1 to 1000" in a distributed cluster).

So anyhow, I'd recommend two things:
1. Try to benchmark with a larger DNN.  Note that we demonstrated in our original blog post that the TF0.12 version of imagenet/inception scaled roughly linearly with the number of nodes (at least for the small scale clusters that we tested with).
2. Try to benchmark non-distributed TensorFlow against distributed TensorFlow (w/o TFoS, just to eliminate the variables).  Note that in our experiments, running with TFoS may add some slight overhead, since we're also running Spark/Hadoop/HDFS on the same nodes (vs. running distributed TF by itself).

And finally, one other thing to consider is that IF you are benchmarking on a shared Hadoop grid, that grid may be running other jobs on the same nodes as well...

Hope that explains my points better...

No, the examples show how to use the `n3` method on `Literal`instances to get a native string representation of the literal in Notation 3 format (which for these objects is equivalent to the Turtle format).

You are not supposed to use this method at all when adding values to a `Graph` instance, as that uses instances of the rdflib term types themselves.

So if you need to add a literal typed with the qudt:Meter datatype, just use the instance created by:

    Literal(2, datatype=URIRef("http://qudt.org/vocab/unit#Meter"))

The method returns `None` if the given resource doesn't have the correct type. 

If it does, it constructs a `Seq` object https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/master/rdflib/graph.py#L1698 
which in turn is iterable. 

Your data has to Seq typed resources, so None is returned!

Hi @gigasquid   CI runs stuff in docker containers.

The steps to add your code to CI would be the following:

1. Add your clojure dependencies to  `ci/docker/install/ubuntu_clojure.sh`     (check how it's done with scala)

2. You need to add one function to
`ci/docker/runtime_functions.sh` that builds your package

and then add a stage into the Jenkinsfile:

See for example this PR which adds an additional Android stage:

https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/11382/files#diff-58231b16fdee45a03a4ee3cf94a9f2c3L486

To test locally run:

ci/build.py --platform ubuntu_cpu --shm-size 500m /work/runtime_functions.sh unittest_ubuntu_cpu_scala

but just use the function for clojure that was just created.

ci/build.py --platform ubuntu_cpu -i      will put you inside the container, so you can check what steps you need to take and add to the runtime_functions.sh script.  

Let me know if you have any issues.

Without `.asscalar()`:

```python
import mxnet as mx
import mxnet.ndarray as nd
import numpy as np
import time

# Warm up the GPU
for _ in range(10):
    a = nd.ones((100, 100), ctx=mx.gpu())
    b = a * 2
    b.asnumpy()

N = 100

# Test the speed
for data_shape in [(16,), (64,), (256,), (1024,)]:
    dat_npy = np.random.uniform(0, 1, data_shape)
    dat_nd_gpu = nd.array(dat_npy, ctx=mx.gpu())
    dat_nd_cpu = nd.array(dat_npy, ctx=mx.cpu())
    nd.waitall()
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(N):
        np_ret = np.sum(dat_npy)
    end = time.time()
    np_time = end - start
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(N):
        nd_ret = nd.sum(dat_nd_gpu)
        nd.waitall()
    end = time.time()
    nd_gpu_time = end - start
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(N):
        nd_ret = nd.sum(dat_nd_cpu)
        nd.waitall()
    end = time.time()
    nd_cpu_time = end - start
    print('sum, data_shape=%s, numpy time=%g, mxnet gpu time=%g, mxnet cpu time=%g' %(str(data_shape), np_time, nd_gpu_time, nd_cpu_time))
```

@SumNeuron glancing quickly through the issue it seems that you want to format your data in the following way
`(N, C, T)` where `N` is the batch size, `C` the number of channels and `T` the length of signal time window (sometimes called `W` for width of your signal).
For example, for 3 channels and 64 measurements, using 32 convolutions kernels:
```python
N = 1
T = 64
C = 3
signal = mx.nd.ones((N, C, T))
conv1D = mx.gluon.nn.Conv1D(channels=32, kernel_size=3)
conv1D.initialize()
out = conv1D(signal)

out.shape
(1, 32, 62)
```

It took some time because I had to figure out how to properly test the warning-system in Python 2.7 https://docs.python.org/2/library/warnings.html:  One thing to be aware of is that if a warning has already been raised because of a once/default rule, then no matter what filters are set the warning will not be seen again unless the warnings registry related to the warning has been cleared.
I got the solution here:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19428761/python-showing-once-warnings-again-resetting-all-warning-registries

Therefore I had to add some code to properly reset the warning system to do the test. It is in now and everything is green. If there are any more questions, please let me know. If now, let me know if we're good to merge.

I wonder if "DirectX SDK won't install properly to start..." is the stupid "[s1023 error](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/kb/2728613)"
* C++ 2010 Redistributable packages are already installed on your computer
* DirectX SDK tries to install these itself and doesn't receive "new package installed"
	* since they're already installed
* The DirectX SDK installation gives up

The recommend solution (which I've used at least three times) is to remove these specific Redistributable packages then run the installer which will install these on its own. [The knowledge base article](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/kb/2728613) suggests these two commands

	MsiExec.exe /passive /X{F0C3E5D1-1ADE-321E-8167-68EF0DE699A5}
	MsiExec.exe /passive /X{1D8E6291-B0D5-35EC-8441-6616F567A0F7}

I know that it's not what you wanted, (pre-built binaries) but maybe it gets you closer to what you need?

(In my experience, CMake tries to dynamically link the CRT anyway which is "real fun" when co-workers don't have the same versions installed)

Nit (feel free to correct if I am wrong!): Division by zero is well-defined by IEEE 754 and caused +INF or -INF depending on the signs involved. Any further calculation involving INFs then causes NAN.

Generally, degenerate geometry is very frequent and avoiding any numerical issues in a codebase this large (and so much not focussed on getting computational geometry right ..) is hard. There is no right way of handling a non-existent normal or a degenerate tangent space. A zero vector is as much a problem as INF or NAN: if it propagates, it kills lighting calculations somewhere in the frontend :-) We have a postprocess step that detects and removes any such stuff. Unfortunately it runs before triangulation though.

You've got me, the pycharm argument was a good reason, not the true one. Moreover my "PEP8 example" had more many blank lines than required by PEP8. (I've posted a corrected version at the end)

The point is that I could see your library being widely adopted I love the idea of an accessible deep learning library that can be used by *regular* programmers, with a sensible set of defaults. But a *regular* python programmer is used to PEP8, and it is hard to shake off, especially when most of the python libraries (including TF & pytorch) follow this style to some extent. So there will be a constant tension, that I'd like to minimize.

The argument about autoformatting was a bad turn, It was just to give you a good rationale to talk about the styles without going into the discussion around personal tastes and adoption. You are right formatting the code automatically won't work and I wasn't even thinking about it.

I meant configuring linters to return warnings that you truly want to fix. When It returns 8k warnings it isn't helpful. If we configure the pep8 linter then we can use it to create a consistent style for the project that can be learned by contributors, it autocorrect the code.

To create a consistent style we can see what types of warnings we have in the code and decide what to correct and which checks to disable:
```
$ pep8 --statistics -qq . | sort -nr
3110    E231 missing whitespace after ','
1938    W191 indentation contains tabs
669     E501 line too long (83 > 79 characters)
619     E261 at least two spaces before inline comment
266     E302 expected 2 blank lines, found 1
188     E701 multiple statements on one line (colon)
164     E225 missing whitespace around operator
142     E128 continuation line under-indented for visual indent
130     E111 indentation is not a multiple of four
55      E301 expected 1 blank line, found 0
54      W293 blank line contains whitespace
22      W391 blank line at end of file
20      E265 block comment should start with '# '
13      W291 trailing whitespace
10      E401 multiple imports on one line
9       E202 whitespace before ']'
8       E114 indentation is not a multiple of four (comment)
6       E266 too many leading '#' for block comment
6       E221 multiple spaces before operator
6       E127 continuation line over-indented for visual indent
5       E201 whitespace after '('
4       E702 multiple statements on one line (semicolon)
4       E402 module level import not at top of file
4       E116 unexpected indentation (comment)
4       E101 indentation contains mixed spaces and tabs
3       W503 line break before binary operator
3       W292 no newline at end of file
3       E303 too many blank lines (2)
3       E272 multiple spaces before keyword
3       E211 whitespace before '['
3       E203 whitespace before ':'
2       E251 unexpected spaces around keyword / parameter equals
2       E122 continuation line missing indentation or outdented
1       E712 comparison to True should be 'if cond is True:' or 'if cond:'
1       E711 comparison to None should be 'if cond is None:'
1       E703 statement ends with a semicolon
1       E502 the backslash is redundant between brackets
1       E271 multiple spaces after keyword
1       E228 missing whitespace around modulo operator
1       E222 multiple spaces after operator
1       E124 closing bracket does not match visual indentation
```

I guess you don't care about the infrequent issues and we can assume that all of the should be corrected. So let's talk  about the most frequrent issues:

> 3110    E231 missing whitespace after ','  

I guess you don't mind having that corrected. For me, it is a sign of clean code so I would love to see it fixed.

> 1938    W191 indentation contains tabs

I bet you had your reasoning for that, and I don't think ppl will care too much about this. Although the reason why PEP recommends spaces is to avoid having it mixed with spaces as this can introduce errors that are hard to notice, especially for newbies.  Correcting this would be a pain so I would keep it. I think there will be another warning if the tabs and spaces are mixed, so this warning can be safely disabled.

> 669     E501 line too long (83 > 79 characters)

I bet no-one cares 4 characters. The only reason to correct this is to be able to enforce the 80c rule in the future.

> 619    E261 at least two spaces before inline comment

That seems to be caused by commented code or the alignment you were talking about. I would turn that warning off.

> 266     E302 expected 2 blank lines, found 1

This is at the end of the classes, I would love that to be corrected. It helps me locate places where a class ends.

> 188     E701 multiple statements on one line (colon)

That is one major readability issue for me, I think it is the second reason after E231 why I started this issue.

> 164     E225 missing whitespaces around operator

I looked at few examples and I guess you are fine to get that corrected. Here they are:
- `*xs,y=args`  should become:
   `*xs, y = args`
- `self.n_emb, self.n_cont=n_emb, n_cont` should become
   `self.n_emb, self.n_cont = n_emb, n_cont`

> 142     E128 continuation line under-indented for visual indent

The `SOVERSION` is only the major version (the equivalent of `LT_CURRENT`). It is called this way because this is the only part actually encoded in `DT_SONAME`. This is the version that you should bump if and only if the ABI changes, as a bump forces everything dependent on the library to be rebuilt. In libtool, this must be a single integer. CMake allows you to set `SOVERSION` to anything, but it is typically an integer as in libtool.

The `VERSION` is the full version, with which the real file (as opposed to a symlink) is installed. In the libtool scheme, it is of the *major.minor.revision* form, where the minor version is actually calculated through a subtraction in libtool. CMake actually allows you to set `VERSION` to anything. Here, a classic *major.minor.revision* is used.

The purpose of NOINLINE is to prohibit any kind of inlining for medium-sized functions that are called from templated code, or simply from many different places. (for trivial ones we don't care, and large-sized functions wouldn't actually get inlined). ``get_internals()`` definitely satisfies that criterion. A function call is fairly compact and leads to considerable object code savings in this case. I was able to shave off quite a bit throughout the development of this project.

```python
import requests_ftp
import datetime
requests_ftp.monkeypatch_session()
import requests
import requests_cache
from pandas.io.common import ZipFile
from pandas.compat import BytesIO


# session = requests.Session()

_URL = 'ftp://ftp.sec.gov/edgar/full-index/master.zip'

s = requests_cache.CachedSession(cache_name='cache', backend='sqlite',
                                 expire_after=datetime.timedelta(days=60))
response = s.get(_URL)
content = response.content

content = BytesIO(content)

with ZipFile(content, 'r') as zf:

with open('master.txt', 'w') as fd:
    fd.write(index_file)

Some time back I wrote some fortran routines to handle despeckling and defreckling, which I then converted into python modules using f2py. I did not reference the manner in which solo handles these procedures, but I expect they may be similar. I did however perform direct comparisons between solo and my code, and the results are nearly identical (see the comparison below, which also includes a few additional edits such as thresholding on NCP).

I implemented a freckle average threshold parameter which allows for the definition of a freckle to be easily changed (by considering the average of adjacent gates as compared to the target gate). I had plans to also implement the ability to easily redefine the maximum speckle size, but never got around to it (currently hard coded to a max of 3 consecutive gates).

Currently, both 1d and 2d approach are used for both the despeckling and defreckling routines. The 1d just looks at adjacent gates in each ray. The 2d considers the same gate number as the target, but in adjacent rays (so not truly 2d, but on the right track).

I have the intention of making the code far more efficient (it currently relies heavily on loops), and to improve the 2d algorithms, but am currently too busy with other projects.

One of the parent python scripts and all of the fortran code (and resultant modules) are available here: https://uofi.box.com/s/2oksf6npr5eac5mq9x22lnrc8ymtc0l0

The script is reasonably commented and/or self-explanatory, but please let me know if anything is unclear if you choose to adopt any of this.

Unfortunately, I'm not really sure if and what to do about it -- in the case of MNIST digits, replacing out-of-sample pixels with zero is an obvious solution because the background is black, for other images this may not be suitable at all. We could add a border mode argument to the layer to specify what should happen with out-of-sample pixels (clip to border as we do now, fill with a given constant, mirror the input, or repeat the input). `scipy.ndimage` has this for many of its filters:
```
mode : {'reflect', 'constant', 'nearest', 'mirror', 'wrap'}, optional
    The `mode` parameter determines how the array borders are
    handled, where `cval` is the value when mode is equal to
    'constant'. Default is 'reflect'
cval : scalar, optional
    Value to fill past edges of input if `mode` is 'constant'. Default
    is 0.0
```
Mirroring and wrapping are trivial modifications of the current code (replacing `T.clip` with other operations), replacing by a constant is more involved. Maybe it's actually a good workaround to pad the image with a border of the value you'd like. In any case, a PR adding a `border_mode` argument or similarly would be welcome!

Just so you know the implementation already works :) and yes it does enforce strict mode.

> > The current interface hides these debug tools from the user

> I don't see how.

Setting the test_value on the input is easy, accessing it on the other layers is complicated. If you have a network with many layers and want to step through it and check the output of each layer to potentially intervene manually you have to call `get_output` on each of them. 

Say you have a big model that takes a very long time to compile (which is especially a problem with test_values). Suppose you have multiple bugs: you'd rather patch each of them temporarily (take note of the bug, change the test_value to the expected value and step to the next line) than kill the job, fix one bug and compile everything again.

> When do you need to set constructor parameters of the next layer depending on the symbolic shape (and how can you actually do so, if you've got the symbolic shape only)? Do you have a concrete use case?

One example could be a contractive-decontractive model with transposed convolution. Transposed convolution is a one-to-many operation. To select one of the many possible output shapes what I usually do is to save the symbolic shape of the each layer in the contractive pathway and pass it as a parameter to the constructor of the corresponding upsampling layer. To do so, I modified Lasagne's layer to accept an extra parameter and pass it to the underlying theano (gradient) op. Having a separate Layer to do the padding in this case would be possible but not ideal, because it would introduce new nodes in the graph instead of fully exploiting the arguments of the gradient op.

> Now l_3 might depend on both l_in.input_var and the new variable. How'd you fix that? Cloning l_3.get_output() with replacement instead of calling with the new variable?

I think this is an extreme example. Is there a use case where it would make sense to do something like this instead of creating the `InputLayer` with the right theano variable in the first place? Anyway, for the sake of the argument, I guess I would call with the new variable.

> When would you start to precompute expressions? Whenever a layer is added or only when get_output() is called? 

I would precompute expressions only when `get_output()` is called. The goal is to allow for multiple calls to `get_output`, but I don't see a reason to precompute something until it's being requested.

> What if get_output() with default arguments fails because some layer class chooses to alter its behaviour in a way that requires certain keyword arguments?

I am not completely sure I am following, but I guess this refers to the case where you precompute everything. If you stick to just caching the intermediate outputs when `get_output()` is called, the user should know which keyword arguments to provide when he calls the function.

I fixed it by "unpickling" the model like this in Python 3.4:

```
with open(model_name, "rb") as f:
    model = pickle.load(f, encoding="latin1")
```

(I was using `encoding="bytes"` before, which produced the error I reported above)

Well, it does complain when the expression is built (concretely, [in this line](https://github.com/Theano/Theano/blob/rel-0.8.2/theano/sandbox/cuda/dnn.py#L419)):
```
TypeError: CudaNdarrayType only supports dtype float32 for now. Tried using dtype float64 for variable None 
```
It's just that Theano's complaint is not very user-friendly, because it relates to trying to convert the input expression to the `Conv2DDNNLayer` (which has a name of `None`) to a `CudaNdarray`. And if floatX=float64, there's hardly any way to have the input expression remain float32, users would have to create all variables and parameters manually.
There is a way, though, and also it's possible somebody makes a case distinction in their code to not use `Conv2DDNNLayer` when floatX=float64, so I'd make it a warning, not an error. Nothing bad happens when you merely *import* the `dnn` module.

The alternative would be to throw an exception in `get_output_for()` of all cuDNN-based layers, when we can check whether the input and/or weights are double precision.

@microamp Thanks, this is a great idea. There's currently no documentation on this, but to my knowledge the main differences are the specifics of the Python API and PyKafka's implementation of the `BalancedConsumer`. PyKafka strives to keep the API as pythonic as possible, which means using useful features of the language where appropriate for client code simplicity. This includes things like context managers for object cleanup and futures for asynchronous error handling. PyKafka's balanced consumer implements the Kafka project's notion of the "high level consumer", which uses ZooKeeper to balance consumption of partitions between multiple nodes in a consumer group. From what I understand, kafka-python is waiting until Kafka 0.9, when this functionality will be supported natively by the Kafka server itself,  to implement self-balancing consumers.
Also, the last time we did a speed test (which was admittedly a while ago at this point), PyKafka's consumer outperformed kafka-python. I unfortunately no longer have the results from that test, so you may not want to bet too hard on PyKafka being significantly faster or slower - just figured I'd mention it.

Whether the vocabulary has padding and OOV tokens or not is configurable, and thus really orthogonal to this question - just change the namespace of the token indexer to something that is not padded by default, or add a non-padded namespace.  Not being able to separate padding tokens from OOV tokens, and just have padding but no OOV, is indeed a little annoying, but also orthogonal to the question - that's handled by the `Vocabulary`, not by the `Field`, and you couldn't easily write _any_ `Field` code to make that happen; you'd need to change `Vocabulary` code.

So the only remaining objection is that `TextField` takes `Token` objects instead of strings.  Yeah, that's a little bit different, but I don't think it's a blocking issue.

What I'm saying now might seem a little inconsistent with what I said at the beginning of this PR.  Here's why I think these situations are different: in the first case, you were proposing _changing_ a `Field's` code to make it semantically ambiguous in a way that's pretty jarring to me.  In this case, I'm suggesting _using_ an existing `Field` to do something it was already designed to do, just in a constrained setting.  The caller has to be sure that the `Field` is constructed in a way that's consistent with its expectations, but other than that, the `Field` is just working as intended.  So this seems fine to me, and not really worth adding another abstraction that is just a restricted version of an abstraction we already have.

That's brittle - it relies on us never using the vocabulary to process data before constructing the model object.  That's currently true in our train command, but not in, e.g., `make-vocab`.  It also could lead to some really subtle bugs, as we currently _save_ the vocabulary right after constructing it, before it gets passed to the model - because of how `train` works, you'd actually index things correctly, but you'd be super confused if you actually looked at the vocab files.

Ideally, you really want the vocabulary to be finalized before the model uses it to set dimensions and things.  This is tricky if you want the model to influence the vocabulary construction, however.

Alternatively, you could make use of Managed Policy and not have an extra resource in here:

```yaml
    LambdaRole:
      Type: "AWS::IAM::Role"
      Properties:
        # Managed Policy include required Lambda Execution Role permissions
        ManagedPolicyArns:
            - "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole"
        AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
          Version: "2012-10-17"
          Statement:
            -
              Sid: "AllowLambdaServiceToAssumeRole"
              Effect: "Allow"
              Action:
                - "sts:AssumeRole"
              Principal:
                Service:
                  - "lambda.amazonaws.com"

    Lambda:
      Type: "AWS::Serverless::Function"
      Properties:
        Role: !GetAtt LambdaRole.Arn
        Handler: lambda_function.lambda_handler
        Runtime: python2.7
        Timeout: 15
        CodeUri: "s3://..."
```

Removed ``DependsOn`` in ``Serverless::Function`` as it's not supported and merged Policy and Role by making use of ``AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole`` that does exactly the same that IAM policy above.

If nginx isn't already running, it uses nginx -c <path to nginx server root>. I'm assuming at least the former command doesn't fail with this setup? 

The former command returns `0` (success; eg the signal was sent), but the reload fails -- permission errors appear in my nginx log.  My suggestion is that Certbot could testing the PIDs of the nginx worker processes.  Nginx's [docs](http://nginx.org/en/docs/control.html) imply this is the only way to know.  Upon a successful reload the `nginx: worker process` processes are replaced and thus their PIDs change; they stay the same otherwise.  In Python, `psutil` could do this:

```python
>>> import psutil
>>> nginx = psutil.Process(25405) #Master
>>> nginx.children()  # Before reload
[<psutil.Process(pid=26609, name='nginx') at 140681397141136>, <psutil.Process(pid=26610, name='nginx') at 140681397141264>]
>>> nginx.children()  # After nginx -s reload in other terminal
[<psutil.Process(pid=27947, name='nginx') at 140681587200080>, <psutil.Process(pid=27948, name='nginx') at 140681397166416>]
```

Certbot's package on RHEL 7 currently depends on psutil so it's already there.

Other non-starter options could be to:
* Test the error log if errors appear immediately after the reload (but that's brittle and people can turn off logging + other errors could conflict) or 
* Test the SNI request locally first (or add a 'dummy' block) to see if nginx was reloaded okay.  This is at risk of a local firewall rule conflicting with this (eg localhost is denied access to its own HTTPS port).

es = Elasticsearch(serializer=MyCustomSerializer)
```

Sorry, that repository has been repurposed. I don't have the instructions up anywhere, so I'll just put them here:

To create CA:
1. Generate new key and cert with password:
   `openssl req -new -x509 -keyout private/cakey.pem -out CA/cacert.pem -days 365 -config ./openssl.cnf`
2. Initialize serial number:
    `echo '100001' > CA/serial`
3. Init cert db:
    `touch CA/certindex.txt`
4. Open CA/cacert.pem on OSX and trust it in the keychain.

To issue new certificate:
1. Generate new RSA private key:
    `openssl genrsa -out private/ssl.key 4096`
2. Make a new certificate request:
    `openssl req -new -out ./ssl.req -key private/ssl.key -config ./openssl.cnf`
3. (optional) Check request for Subject Alternative Name info (SAN)
    `openssl req -text -noout -in ./ssl.req`
4. Issue new cert from CA:
    `openssl ca -in ssl.req -out ssl.cert -config openssl.cnf -extensions v3_req`

That's a difference between Legacy Python and Python 3. In Legacy Python, calling `range()` returns a list of numbers. In Python 3, it creates a `range` object, which is more efficient, and whose representation happens to look the same as your input. You can see this more clearly if you do:

```python
start = 3
stop = 12
range(start, stop)
```

which will have the result

```python
range(3, 12)
```

If you want to get the Legacy Python list in Python 3, you can pass the range to `list`:

```python
list(range(2, 5))
[2, 3, 4]
```

Recently I have been looking at the UX design around RStudio's document conversion stuff. I knmow some folks who use Jupyter and RStudio and from their perspective, the UX of RStudio is so far ahead of us on this point, they wouldn't think of using nbconvert - even though nbconvert does much of what is offered on RStudio from the technical perspective.

What is a remarkable is that from a technical perspective, what RStudio does is much more primitive that what we do in the notebook (custom REST endpoint, menus, custom UI). They have nicer UI, but the result of that UI is to just fire off a command in the terminal. It converts things in place and leaves the converted documents (HTML, pdf, etc.) in that directory.

I think we should step back from the technical details of this and do some UX design work to understand what Jupyter users really need in this area. It may be that we can provide what they need with some small UI improvements and no difficult technical work.

I think studying the RMarkdown/RStudio model would be a good starting point.

hey @sylvainKritter ! The algorithm to detect is based on the method used by CTP - there is a list of "known" locations in the deid recipe (see [here for the default](https://github.com/pydicom/deid/blob/master/deid/data/deid.dicom#L109)) and then they are blacked out with this function here: https://github.com/pydicom/deid/blob/master/deid/dicom/pixels/detect.py#L81 Matching to the recipe is based on header fields, so it follows that if your data isn't represented there, or the headers have been changed, it won't work well. A much better solution here would be to develop something that uses character recognition (and do away with the manual aspect), and this is a project I had wanted to do but I didn't have anyone to work with. I'll write the detail here in case someone reading (now or future) is interested in this work. We would want to:

 - generate a large set of labeled images (phi vs. not)
 - develop an OCR method (and actually I had started but didn't continue --> https://github.com/pydicom/dicom-scraper
 - test our method and compare to the manual, etc.

Ideally there would be a method that can be tested and then some actual numbers shown to various IRB boards so it could be approved. I had wanted to do this but I can only take on so much on my own! If you (or a group that you know) would be interested in this work let me know! For now I'll merge the PR, upload to pypi, and add the zenodo record. Thanks for finding the issue!

Fair enough. Indeed the `__slots__` are more of a class-level attribute and it cannot be changed on instances after they are created so yeah it probably is better to have them on the class, not on the instance. :+1:

Interestingly, I was trying to "break" Python but it actually ends up behaving in a rather weird way:

```python
import sys
print(sys.version)

class Foo(object):
	  __slots__ = ("hello",)

	  def __init__(self):
	  	  self.hello = "howdy"

Foo.__slots__ = ("world",)

f1 = Foo()
print(dir(f1))

print(f1.hello)
print(Foo.__slots__)
```

The result is not what I was expecting:

```
3.6.3 (default, Oct  3 2017, 21:45:48) 
[GCC 7.2.0]
['__class__', '__delattr__', '__dir__', '__doc__', '__eq__', '__format__', '__ge__', '__getattribute__', '__gt__', '__hash__', '__init__', '__init_subclass__', '__le__', '__lt__', '__module__', '__ne__', '__new__', '__reduce__', '__reduce_ex__', '__repr__', '__setattr__', '__sizeof__', '__slots__', '__str__', '__subclasshook__', 'hello']
howdy
('world',)
```

So it seems like you can change the `__slots__` on the class after it is created, but Python will ignore your change when creating new instances.

So in this very specific case the memory accounting will probably be off because `Foo.__slots__` is actually not the same as `f1.__slots__` ;)
But we may just not want to care about such corner cases.

The case I met in my job:
- TaskA requires TaskB and TaskC
- TaskB is not directly dependent of TaskC but must be executed after the TaskC

Then you need this order: TaskC -> TaskB -> TaskA

It's wrong to consider than TaskA can require both TaskB and TaskC and then order their execution with the priority (as I described previously, it's not safe with multiple workers).

You must have: TaskA requires TaskB. TaskB requires TaskC.
By this you will be sure that TaskC will be executed before TaskB.

Here's what I have that works with multiple handlers and multiple workers, maybe you can work backwards from this:

```
import logging
import luigi

LOGGER = logging.getLogger(__name__)
LOGGER.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
LOGGER.addHandler(logging.StreamHandler())
LOGGER.addHandler(logging.FileHandler('output.log'))

class LoggingTask(luigi.Task):
    item = luigi.Parameter()

    def requires(self):
       LOGGER.debug("in requires")
       return

    def output(self):
        LOGGER.debug("in output")
        return luigi.LocalTarget("output" + str(self.item))

    def run(self):
        LOGGER.debug("in run")
        output = open("output" + str(self.item), "w")
        output.close()


class ParentTask(luigi.WrapperTask):

    def requires(self):
        LOGGER.debug("in parent task requires")
        child_tasks = []
        for i in range(0,4):
            child_tasks.append(LoggingTask(item=str(i)))
        return child_tasks
```

run at command line with:

`PYTHONPATH=. luigi --module logging_test ParentTask --workers 4`

`            all_vars = tf.get_collection(tf.GraphKeys.VARIABLES)
`
to
`            all_vars = tf.get_collection(tf.GraphKeys.GLOBAL_VARIABLES)
`
in `tflearn.helpers.trainer.py` then `model.fit()` and `model.save()` worked fine with no errors.
I got some warnings and errors when I loaded model though.
They all seem to related with the recently updated variable names. E.g. I got an error of these

```
WARNING:tensorflow:From /Users/jinsung/env/tensorflow012/lib/python3.5/site-packages/tflearn/helpers/trainer.py:429 in restore.: initialize_all_variables (from tensorflow.python.ops.variables) is deprecated and will be removed after 2017-03-02.
Instructions for updating:
Use `tf.global_variables_initializer` instead.

```
```
WARNING:tensorflow:Error encountered when serializing summary_tags.

'dict' object has no attribute 'name'
WARNING:tensorflow:Error encountered when serializing data_preprocessing.

```
