I want to just click the green button here, but years of setuptools FUD have made me fearful and distrustful of `setup_requires`.  @dstufft, am I now a fool for holding on to this fear, or merely recalling hard-won wisdom?

`for c in children` will process the list items in the same order.  From the Python docs[1]:

> Python’s for statement iterates over the items of any sequence (a list or a string), **in the order** that they appear in the sequence.

(emphasis is mine).

Under normal conditions, the order that results are returned from an LDAP search are undefined.  I don't have the RFC reference for this, though.  It is just an observation I have made.

[1] https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/controlflow.html#for-statements

sat.parallel_threads is an early version of portfolio support in the sat solver. 
I suggest to ignore it for now because it will be refined in a different branch.

It will be replaced in a later check-in (4+ months away) by a portfolio approach that is similar to 
other SAT solvers. The opt branch in https://GitHub.com/nikolajbjorner/z3.git contains the development. 
It gives a modest speedup, mainly as it enables the first-finisher to report the verdict.
The threads exchange lemmas, and you will have an option to include local search (to find satisfying assignments) in case this helps. The benefit on smaller formulas is questionable as there is some overhead with spawning the threads, so the sequential version may do very well in many cases. 
par-or or application level portfolio may also be good options depending on your application.

@kovidgoyal I'm not sure the original intention of the parser was to fall back to parsing ISO dates if the `dayfirst` option was not specified - I think that was an unintended consequence. I agree, however, that there's certainly an identifiable need to be able to provide some interface which allows downstream users to handle fallback ordering as desired.

As I've mentioned on other issues (such as #125 and #214, among others), I think the way forward is to do two things:

1.  Provide an output format that can be re-used to parse strings of the same type, with a public interface for querying the string ordering in some identifiable way (this way you can parse, determine if the string was parsed in a way you didn't expect, then parse again with different options if need be). I think this is useful in general (so you can, for example, "cache" the parse operation), but not the most efficient way to handle the problem.

2.   Provide a public interface for tweaking the preference ordering for ambiguous dates. I'm definitely looking for suggestions on how this should look, but I'm thinking that it will start with a refactor of `parser.parser` to break out the monolithic `parse` function into small, modular functions (which can be individually adjusted in subclasses), and also to move things like ambiguity resolution of individual tokens into some instance and/or class parameters, like, `date_tokens = ['mdy', 'myd', 'dmy', 'ymd']`, or even some variation on `strftime` syntax like `%Y%^%m%^%d` where `%^` would be a generic substitute for "any separator".

I do not think that a fundamental parser overhaul will be in the 2.6.0 release (the time zone improvements so far are significant and once they are done I see no reason to delay a major release for as long as it would take to decide on an interface and then implement it. My current plan is to finish the 2.6.0 release, then start on the parser modularization, then I'll open an issue requesting comments on the implementation details of how to specify fallback options.

> "Can you explain your thinking on this a little? Is there some context in which "US/Pacific" etc are incorrect? Or is it just less elegant than the implementation in #227?

I'm not interested in hard-coding *any* sort of data (with certain rare exceptions like `Z` and `UTC`, which are hard-coded into many actual *standards*). It's an improper separation of concerns, we should provide interfaces to data in well-known formats and try to be agnostic about the contents of the data. I'm not even particularly comfortable with the fact that we hard-code the days of the week (though there are many reasons why I'm not enthused about moving away from this at this point).

Rather than hard-code anything, we should have a generalized way to register lists of time zones, and by default we can construct the `parser` instance with a list that includes the IANA database names. (`tzinfos` is close, but it's really only intended for mapping shortnames to zones).

I guess what I really want is to be able to have a simple instruction that tells the system to arm that I can wait on until arming is confirmed to succeed. It should have (optional) debug output on what we are waiting on and a time timeout or fail retry timout.

So something like the below, where debug rate is the spacing between printing conditions you're waiting on to console (None for don't print), retry is how many times it will try resend the arm command after EKF warmup is complete. 
```
arm(await=True, retry=3, debug_rate=None)
```

@fubuloubu very open to working with you to figure out where the pain points are for your use case and how we can make those easier.

Here is a long winded reply that hopefully answers this question.  The roadmap for the coming year involves these four high level things.

**step 1** is going to be an *under-the-hood* improvement in how populus serializes compiled assets and deployed assets.  The new serialization will be the same format that smart contract packages use which will functionally make every one of these assets a *package*.

**step 2** is this issue.  Taking advantage of the new serialization format to fix some long standing deficiencies as well as generally improve the contract APIs.  This includes the python APIs such as Providers and Registrars, as well as command line APIs like this issue describes.

**step 3** will be full support for packaging.  This will include functionality for deploying your own package registrar, creating and publishing a package from your project's contracts, and installing other packages for use in your project.

**step 4** is where I think your problems get addressed.  Command line deployment is fine for relatively simple deployments.  Python code based deployments are fine for when people want full control.  There's a middle road between these two which is some level of automation but not requiring you to do everything.  It's not clear at this point exactly what this will look like, but my best guess is that it will look something like django migrations and management commands.  The ability for you to write scripts that are easy to run against your project and deployed assets as well as batteries included automation for things like deployments.

---------------------------------------------

With respect to how you organize your source files:

Populus currently doesn't mind the folder structure you proposed and should do fine finding all of your contracts.  This however doesn't really help when a project just has a ton of contracts.  The package management features are the best solution I know of for this.  It will allow you to split parts of your project out into stand-alone packages and simply install them in your project.  All of the source files, deployed assets, etc will be available for use, but your project won't be cluttered with extra source files and what-not.

Should `Web` be the root?  The answer to that is that it depends on how self-contained you consider the `Web` dir to be.  If you have build/tooling dependencies that rely on things under `MyApp` that are outside of `Web`, then I'd suggest just taking `MyApp` as the root.

Otherwise: If you want the `Web` dir to be seen as the root for a project not managed in source control, you'll need to provide a hint to watchman.  The `.watchmanconfig` file is the (literally) definitive way to identify a project root; it takes precedence over any of the files that you might choose to list in the `root_files` configuration.  You can, of course, choose to populate your `root_files` configuration on your system to list out the files that you consider to be indicative of a project root.

We're rather selective about what goes in the default `root_files`, because making it match too many things is more limiting than making it match fewer.  It would break several projects at FB if we adopted the list that you suggest above.

Regarding setting things by environment, or otherwise making it a parameter at watch time that is out of band of statically located information from the configuration files; that results in non-deterministic behavior for starting a watch and that is something that we want to avoid in tools.

It sounds like the two least intrusive options for you are:

1. Create a file containing `{}` in `/path/to/MyApp/Clients/Web/.watchmanconfig`, and run `watchman watch-project /path/to/MyApp/Clients/Web`.  This will generate a watch on that dir directly (although if any of the parent dirs are already being watched it will then return that dir and the offset to your web dir) and will work with other tools that use `watch-project` to consolidate watches (those tools include things like Buck, Nuclide, React-Native, ember-cli).
2. You can just do `watchman watch /path/to/MyApp/Clients/Web`.  If you don't need to work with any of the tools I mentioned above, and you don't employ the `root_restrict_files` configuration locally or in any of the places that your `Web` project will be worked on, then you can happily continue to use `watch` in this way.   We consider it deprecated in favor of `watch-project`, but per https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/compatibility.html, it is going to be available and work the same way forever.

I'd encourage you to go with option 1 if you're likely to share that project, but otherwise, go wild and enjoy your freedom to use option 2 :-)

> This will create discrepancy ? Am I right here ? 

You're right splitting a sequence into multiple sequences might lead to different estimates.

> Secondly, Is it possible to cascade the training results to next data ? Is it possible to do like that ?

Yes, you can just call `fit` again on an already trained HMM, but don't forget to set `init_params` to `""`, otherwise the parameter would be re-initialized from the new data.

Getting back to the original issue, I came around a [paper](http://www.machinelearning.org/proceedings/icml2005/papers/101_FastInference_SiddiqiMoore.pdf) which gives an algorithm for learning HMMs with large state space. In a nutshell, the idea is to only estimate the transition probabilities which are "large enough". This gives a reduction in both estimation time and memory consumption. I will see if we could incorporate a similar heuristic into the current implementation.

The good news is that even if we don't get to implement the algorithm from the paper, we could still reduce memory consumption a bit. TBC in a commit message ;)

Oh, I see. It works on Windows with WinKerberos, just not on Linux with PyKerberos. I think I can explain. WinKerberos uses Microsoft's SSPI, which is tightly integrated with Microsoft's entire authentication stack. There is no kinit or anything like it on Windows. The credentials for the process owner are used by default, or to authenticate a different user you use the principal option with a password.

On Linux the underlying technology is MIT krb5, which uses kinit to acquire ticket granting tickets for one or more users. You have to call kinit there first.

I'll also mention that the comment:

```
# This is necessary to support use cases where multiple services
# are hosted on the same machine under multiple CNAMEs. It won't do
# any good to use a SPN that mentions one of the CNAMES, we need to ask for the real machine.
```

is ... indicative of where the disagreement comes from.

When I am sending a request & want to authenticate to a service, I don't care what the "real machine" is. I care that the service which responds is the service I requested, and so if the service doesn't have access to the key which it could use to prove that it is the service that  I expect it to be, I want a mutual authentication error.

With this patch in place, if I want to send a request to trusted.com, and someone wants to pretend to be trusted.com to me, all they'd need to do is find a way to get me to receive a CNAME for trusted.com to redirect me to malicious.com instead. I could then successfully authenticate against malicious.com. All malicious.com would have to do is be able to prove that it was malicious.com for me to trust them. I'd be none-the-wiser.

Also, with apache & nginx (and many other web servers which support kerberos), if they're configured with a single keytab file that has access to keys for all of their named virtual hosts, they'll pick the right one based on the Host header on the request, so it does do good to use the SPN that mentions the CNAME, not the real machine.

If you store someplace the identity and datetime, then you can at any later time get back to that object with something like:
```
entity = MyModel.objects.as_of(desired_datetime).get(identity=desired_identity)
```

And from this entity you can get all the related objects as they were at ``desired_datetime`` just by accessing them as you normally would with the Django ORM, for example:
```
entity.related_thing_set.all()
entity.related_fk_thing
```

If you want to query several entities *at different times* all in one query: no, that is not possible.  You'd need to construct a separate query for each different time.

I don't think there's a singular answer here.  It comes down to a broader question of "what are you trying to protect".

 - In healthcare (my immediate concern), we're protecting the information itself.  We don't distinguish between data generated by the system and data being entered by an individual.  It's all health information needing protected.
 - In other situations, it may be the server session that is of greater concern.  For example, your bank may not care that your balance is still displayed on the screen.  Rather, they're focused on preventing a user from transferring money out of that account.

There are probably intermediate cases too.  For example, a password field should probably be reset in the second case.   Similarly, it might make sense to flag some form fields (e.g. SSN) as "protected".

Hi Bertfried,

- I definitely agree with splitting the domains up in 4 different dictionaries. They could be in a separate module named `domains.py` or something.
- In countries with multiple bidding zones, only the country code will not do. Nothing we can do but to ask the user to be specific.
- About the timezones, I tried this:
```python
import pytz
from entsoe.entsoe import TIMEZONE_MAPPINGS
for code in TIMEZONE_MAPPINGS:
    print(pytz.country_timezones(code.split('-')[0])
```
You see that this approach could work for all country codes, except Russia...
- I don't agree with returning timezone-naive data. It adds ambiguity where there is none. We could however add a flag `localized=False` or something, so you can get data in UTC.
- [You can export any Pandas DataFrame to Json](https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/generated/pandas.DataFrame.to_json.html) 
- I haven't added a dictionary to convert psrTypes yet, so right now you have to use the code as input. Codes are in the documentation in [section A5](https://transparency.entsoe.eu/content/static_content/Static%20content/web%20api/Guide.html#_psrtype).
- You can only do the `import entsoe` line if the module is in your PYTHONPATH (Try `import sys; print(sys.path)`. You can do this by either installing it via pip (not a good idea if you are developing is); launching your Jupyter Notebook in the folder right above the module; or [add the path to the module into your pythonpath](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3402168/permanently-add-a-directory-to-pythonpath)
- You have to make a distinction between configuration variables, and global variables. The `URL` and others are globals, but they don't really need to be configurable by a user. This means you can just add them in a separate python file and import them from there if you want.
- You can send me a pull request whenever you have finished anything. Just a tip/request: if you are working on different topics, please create separate branches and pull requests to split them up. This way everything doesn't end up in one big chunk, which is harder to review.
- I have already added the `PT15M` compatibility to version 0.1.13. I agree to have it changed into a dictionary.
- You can assign variables to a Pandas DataFrame like this (as long as the name is not reserved):
```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame()
df.unit = 'kWh'
df.currency = 'CHF'
```
These attributes will only stick to the DataFrame as long as no operations are performed on it. I don't think this is a recommended way, but it does the trick and I've not yet seen anything better.

Thanks. I subclassed `GoogleRefreshTokenClient` instead like this:

```python
class GoogleCredentialsClient(GoogleRefreshTokenClient):
    def __init__(self, creds):
        """Initializes a GoogleAccessTokenClient. """
        self.oauth2credentials = creds
        self.proxy_config = ProxyConfig()

    def Refresh(self):
        # The default behavior of AdwordsClient seems to be to call
        # its OAuth2 client's `Refresh` function on initialization.
        # We override it here and only call Refresh if the token has
        # expired. In production use, even that shouldn't happen,
        # because we will refresh access tokens periodically.

        if (self.oauth2credentials.token_expiry is not None and
                self.oauth2credentials.token_expiry <
                datetime.datetime.utcnow()):
            logger.debug('Force refreshing credentials, since they have '
                         'expired.')
            super(GoogleCredentialsClient, self).Refresh()
```

I added a commit to use [Python warning framework (PEP 230)](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0230/).

By default since Python 2.7, deprecation warnings are hidden by default ([explanation here](https://docs.python.org/2/library/warnings.html#updating-code-for-new-versions-of-python)).

The recommended way to display them is either:
- using the [`python -W` option](https://docs.python.org/2/using/cmdline.html#cmdoption-W)
- using the [`PYTHONWARNINGS`](https://docs.python.org/2/using/cmdline.html#envvar-PYTHONWARNINGS) environment variable

In short it is possible to convert deprecation warnings into exceptions (`python -W error`) or just display them (`python -W default`). This will have to be documented in a section of the doc with a title like "How to upgrade my OpenFisca user code" (any thought @fpagnoux @MattiSG)?

See also the "Command Line Syntax " section of the [PEP 230](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0230/) (sorry there is no direct link to an anchor).

- `git checkout` the commit where the code is in the state you want

You will then be on `master`, with all the changes between master and your state uncommitted. You can then recommit the way you want.

Considering that the utility of SO is proportional to the number of people asking and answering (whereas Slack is quickly counter-proportional), I believe that such an experiment would make sense only by massively directing questions to SO. I would thus suggest the following steps:

- [ ] Ensure main contributors offering support all have an account on StackOverflow by making them write one Q&A pair on a classical OpenFisca problem.
- [ ] Create the OpenFisca tag on StackOverflow.
- [ ] Make the experimentation explicit in Slack `#support`, and suggest that questions are cross-posted in Slack to give them visibility.
- [ ] `/feed subscribe http://stackoverflow.com/feeds/tag?tagnames=openfisca&sort=newest` in `#support` to ensure all questions are visible there.

1. Does not work with `above` lines: printing happens before prompt is shown, so it will be placed on the uppermost line.

4. Right prompt should disappear if user types long enough command line, not look like part of his input.
5. Right prompt must not disappear if user happens to delete something.
6. Right prompt must reappear if user input is no longer lengthy.

It is better to not have any right prompt at all then to have such a bad right prompt. Bash does not have enough features to emulate right prompt on top of anything it has.

okay, then I understand what the problem is - you are actually not memory leaking, but storing all old analysis.
The Session is used for exactly this purpose: be able to add several APKs / DEX files, then work with them, save the session to disk, load it again and so on.
If you are doing some automated analysis, you probably do not want this behaviour - as you are not interested in keeping old stuff, you already have analysed or maybe even stored in some other format (at least I do such stuff).
If you set the session parameter in `AnalayseAPK` to None, the Default session is used - as I fixed that recently, you will actually always get the same session, thus add more and more APKs to it.

What I can, and will do, is to change the behaviour of AnalyzeAPK and AnalyzeDEX to use no session at all, if session=None.

@tkimnguyen based on my understanding of datagridfield, it is using schema.Object to store the data directly inside the contexted object, but as json data, not as an Object data. Therefore, whenever you call the datagridfield property, you should get something like:

```
[{'file': <NamedFile - Object>, ....},
 {'file': <NamedFile - Object>, ....}]
``` 

In my example above, I used `schema.ASCII` with `NamedImageFieldWidget`  to generate a base64 format of the file, which resulted in the following structure of datagridfiled property:::
```
[{'file':  'data:image/jpeg;base64, LzlqLzRBQ...',
  ....
},{
 'file': 'data:image/jpeg;base64, Mzr7LkP0M...',
 ...
}]
```
 
After re-implementing and improving my approach in another project, I realized that I didn't have to use `schema.ASCII` with `NamedImageFieldWidget`  to accomplish the rendering and downloading of the image. All I needed to do was to ensure that the file wasn't overwritten. This was already done by the `sponsors.setter`. Therefore, I changed `schema.ASCII` with `NamedImageFieldWidget` to `NamedBlobImage` field. Afterwards, I used existing plone portal functions to render the image object.

In your case, you would have to find a portal function that generates a download link from a file object or create a poral function that does this. See [Timestamp Download URL](https://docs.plone.org/develop/plone/forms/files.html#timestamped-download-url) in the Plone documentation.

@oberstet :

With this additional event there would be:

start
connectfailure
connect
join
ready
leave
disconnect

connectfailure will fire on each failed connect attempt with the exception raised for the transport failing.

This got lost in meejah's line comments conversation due to pushing additional commits - some reasons for adding this event were:

Yeah further user-side logging of transport failures for one.

If you start a component with multiple transports this will allow user-code to "do something" with the exception raised as each failed connect attempt happens. Without this, if one of your transports is unconnectable but another succeeds and you get a working session, your component.start() error callback won't be called so you'll never know other than by looking in logs one of your transports didn't work.

Also could be handy for one-shot connect attempts with one transport and max_retries = 0. 'connectfailure' handler will fire and you'll know quickly there's a fundamental connection error without having to look at the exception type in the component.start() error callback which could be a session level exception.

A git commit always have access to the original HEAD and information to generate a new commit (e.g. committer, author, message body, blobs). A hook at this stage would generally examine the information for the new commit.

A git push would always have the current ref tip to push and the remote's original ref tip. A hook at this stage would examine the commits that differ between the remote and the pushed refs.

I was waiting for others to comment, I've already voiced my thoughts elsewhere but I'll reiterate them here.

I don't think this is a good idea for a few reasons:

- It's against the idea of sharing configurations with other individuals.  A definition of a hook should live in one place and consumers should references that single place
- It doesn't work for multiple repositories (two of these "phantom" repositories would reference the same hooks.yaml which would introduce available but broken hook ids to end users)
- Installation of these "phantom" hook repositories would be orphaned from their configuration, potentially leading to unexpected behaviour when using pre-commit across multiple repositories on the same machine
- It introduces yet-another-way to do things which would require maintenance

On the note of a central repository of hooks:
- One already exists: http://pre-commit.com/hooks.html or in consumable format http://pre-commit.com/all-hooks.json
- However, depending on this in a runtime situation would introduce a single-point-of-failure and would require organizations which want to use pre-commit to set up an internal "pre-commit repository" (this is a huge barrier to entry for many, especially enterprise).

On the note of non-VCS:
- This again goes against the direction of reusable hook definitions, but it also introduces significant logic into pre-commit to understand each and every package management system and the quirks of each of them

@blink1073 sorry about the delay, just got back to looking at this.  I'm still getting that stack trace when I try to remove a row, but in the process of trying to fix it I came up with a couple of new suggestions:

##### 1. Switch to using latest methods for loading widget JS
I was reading about widget best practices and learned that there's a new strategy for distributing the JS that's associated with a widget, as we need to do here.  Take a look at this paragraph, taken from https://ipython.org/ipython-doc/3/whatsnew/version3_widget_migration.html:

> If you distribute your custom widget, you may be using display and Javascript to publish the widget’s Javascript to the front-end. That is no longer the recommended way of distributing widget Javascript. Instead have the user install the Javascript to his/her nbextension directory or their profile’s static directory. Then use the new _view_module and _model_module traitlets in combination with _view_name and _model_name to instruct require.js on how to load the widget’s Javascript. The Javascript is then loaded when the widget is used for the first time.

I also came across what I think is a good example of this strategy being put to use: https://github.com/takluyver/mobilechelonian

##### 2. Make buttons hidden by default, to reduce UI clutter
I think the widget looks slightly less cool with the Add Row / Remove Row / Export buttons on top of the grid.  I'm thinking ideally we'd make those buttons optional and default to being hidden (i.e. we'd add a new `show_toolbar` option that could be set with the `set_defaults` function, and would default to `False`).

I think I'd be fine merging without 2, but the 1 seems like it's worth doing.  Let me know if you'd like me to help out with either of these changes (although I might not have time for a couple of weeks due an upcoming vacation).

What is worse, it seems  hard to recreate, subtle bug. I failed to recreate it on travis, even with similar set up. Problem is that `max_drawdown` is passing a `Series` object to `np.nanmin`, which in turn is calling `np.fmin.reduce` to do actual aggregation. On my system (numpy 1.12.1; pandas 0.19.2), `np.fmin.reduce` is returning a `Series` object which is being cascaded upward. I couldn't test beyond that but the problem seems to be related to following pandas warning:

> 
Warning
In 0.13.0 since Series has internaly been refactored to no longer sub-class ndarray but instead subclass NDFrame, you can not pass a Series directly as a ndarray typed parameter to a cython function. Instead pass the actual ndarray using the .values attribute of the Series.
> 
So the issue is that pandas API no longer guarantees symmetric treatment of Series and 1-d numpy arrays (unless explicitly done so by passing Series.values); it depends on the implementation. And on my system at least it is returning a Series object, not float scalar, even when input is 1d.

To make the code more robust, two approaches are possible:

- One is to check input before it is being passed to `np.nanmin` and explicitly pass `Series.values`.
- Other is to check output after it being returned by `np.nanmin` and handle the cases.

Both approaches work fine (I've tested both), though my PR is based on second.

I'm just slightly uneasy about dropping the only piece of information indicating that the original input contained a tz offset, when it may or may not have been there.

A less controversial variant of plan B could involve converting tz-aware values to UTC but stopping short of dropping the `tzinfo` objects:

  * value with an offset → `datetime.datetime(<converted datetime>, tzinfo=UTC)`
  * value without an offset → `datetime.datetime(<raw datetime>, tzinfo=None)`

This would preserve the distinction between a local time *n* and UTC time *n*Z while offering some protection against stupid code that ignores the tzinfos.

If the proxy server is not working while using Streamlink but is working while using a different client (your web browser), then you've either misconfigured the Streamlink proxy settings, or the server is blocking Streamlink (which is using the `requests` python library).

Are you sure that you are using the `--http-proxy` parameter correctly?
See this answer of your previous thread(s):
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/233#issuecomment-264007551

The only way of distinguishing between proxy clients is the HTTP headers they are sending (`User-Agent`, etc.) and the IP address. But since you're using the same computer and the same connection, the address will be the same. Maybe you could try replacing the User-Agent header with the [`--http-header`](https://streamlink.github.io/cli.html#cmdoption-http-header) parameter.

By using the [`--json`](https://streamlink.github.io/cli.html#cmdoption-j) parameter, you should be able to see the headers being used. I can't tell you which headers IE is using, though...

## Different .so modules

Suggested by @socketpair. There are three .so files compiled with different flags:

* General version with no additional flags
* General + SSE4 with `-msse4`
* General + SSE4 + AVX2 with `-mavx2`

We are detecting CPUID and load appropriate modules from python code.

Pros:
* Easy switching between different versions (for unit tests or in case of problems with automatic detection)
* Looks like easy building with setuptools

Cons:
* 3x compile time
Version 0.2.2 includes these changes (available via pip).

To clarify where the Cisco docs are confusing:
From [list messages](https://developer.ciscospark.com/endpoint-messages-get.html)
> Limit the maximum number of messages in the response.

But [pagination](https://developer.ciscospark.com/pagination.html) clarifies:
> When requesting a list of resources the max query parameter may be used to control the number of items returned per page.

So like I said, when you use the requests module you are actually getting just the first page of results as the response. The wrapper though will also see that there are additional pages available and is capable of requesting those too. In other words, all the max argument does in the wrapper is change how many requests it'd need to make to get everything. If there are 1000 messages and you set max at 1000 the wrapper makes one request, if you set max at 500 it makes two requests.

I used ambiguity, but the output have no _ambig node for the input `S -> S SS | &`:
```regex
+ productions
+   new_line
+ 
+   non_terminal_start
+     non_terminal  S
+   non_terminals
+     production
+       non_terminal
+         S
+         S
+         S
+     production
+       epsilon
+   end_symbol
+     new_line
```

Grammar:
```regex
    _parser = lark.Lark( r"""
        productions   : new_line* ( non_terminal_start "->" non_terminals end_symbol )* non_terminal_start "->" non_terminals end_symbol?
        non_terminals : production ( "|" production )*
        production    : ( ( epsilon | terminal | non_terminal )+ space+ )* ( epsilon | terminal | non_terminal )+

        // Forces them to appear in the tree as branches
        epsilon         : [] | "&"+
        end_symbol      : ";"* new_line+
        terminal        : ( SIGNED_NUMBER | LCASE_LETTER | dash_phi_hyphen )+
        non_terminal    : UCASE_LETTER+ ( UCASE_LETTER | DIGIT | quote )*
        new_line        : NEWLINE
        quote           : "'"
        dash_phi_hyphen : "-"
        space           : " "

        // Rename the start symbol, so when parsing the tree it is simple to find it
        non_terminal_start : non_terminal

        // Tells the tree-builder to inline this branch if it has only one member
        ?non_terminal_epsilon : non_terminal | epsilon

        // Stops Lark from automatically filtering out these literals from the tree
        null   : "null"
        true   : "true"
        false  : "false"

        // Import common definitions
        %import common.INT
        %import common.DIGIT
        %import common.UCASE_LETTER
        %import common.LCASE_LETTER
        %import common.SIGNED_NUMBER
        %import common.NEWLINE
        %import common.WS_INLINE

        // Set to ignore white spaces
        %ignore WS_INLINE
    """, start='productions', parser='earley', ambiguity="explicit" )
```

A non-extensive test shows it can be fixed by a small change:
```patch
diff --git a/letsencrypt-nginx/letsencrypt_nginx/nginxparser.py b/letsencrypt-nginx/letsencrypt_nginx/nginxparser.py
index 749c009..2df26fe 100644
--- a/letsencrypt-nginx/letsencrypt_nginx/nginxparser.py
+++ b/letsencrypt-nginx/letsencrypt_nginx/nginxparser.py
@@ -37,7 +37,7 @@ class RawNginxParser(object):
         + Group(ZeroOrMore(Group(assignment) | block))
         + right_bracket)
 
-    script = OneOrMore(Group(assignment) | block) + stringEnd
+    script = OneOrMore(Group(assignment) ^ block) + stringEnd
```

Reversing the order of `block` and `Group(assignment)` while preserving `|` also fixes it.

After https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/pull/602 lands I'll do a full test

抱歉斗胆插一句，我觉得你们俩现在讨论的着眼点是不一样的，所以结论也总是对不上，也不可能对的上。

wizardforcel的着眼点在于怎么针对性对抗类似这篇水paper里的这种机器学习算法针对ss训练的模型，场景设计在于“当某些设备不知道这个新轮子的时候，怎样让它的旧模型失效”。这个方法大概就像wizardforcel说的那样，用些小众的东西搞乱特征空间就行了。缺点是如果这个小众的东西变成大众的，就会被重新针对，然后再次发现。所以这类思路个人认为可以用来实现私人代理协议，做完了给自己用。

ccsexyz的着眼点在于当协议公开（类似ss），某些设备已经知道这个轮子是怎么设计的，在这种前提下怎样避免对方用某些启发式规则或者机器学习等任意方法来检测到协议的通信流量。这个问题通用性高了很多，难度当然也比搞傻一个分类器难多了。这个目前看起来还没有特别好的方法，不然圆葱也不会被搞得那么死了。

The straight (curved) line is where the quadratic (linear) method switches results. The difference in squared (or linear) lengths is what decides this: Above each of those boundaries, it costs less to link the left point with the top one (and the right point with the bottom one). Below the boundary, it costs less to connect the left point with the bottom one. 

Along the straight line, you'll find that the *difference* in the squared distances to the top and bottom points is constant; the equivalent is true of the curve, which makes it a hyperbola. 

The boundaries have the orientation that they do because of symmetry. You can convince yourself that they should have that *position* by moving the free point over close to where the fixed point is. So the distance of 1 unit is a coincidence.

Another thing you may think of (in addition to this feature request), is adding something like `m.SData`. Usage would be:

```
m.transaction(..., data = m.SData, ...)
```

Every time the VM queries for an entry in `m.SData` (eg. for the 3rd byte `m.SData[2]`), if it's the first time it generates a fresh `m.SByte`, if it's a subsequent time it uses the originally generated variable. The advantage of this approach is that you no longer need to have ABI compliant code to be able to infer the correct data-width to use for input (and thus you also don't need the Solidity source). Could think of it as a "lazy infinite list of `m.SByte`

Offset -1001 is a special value meaning Invalid or Default, depending on context.
If you pass a TopicPartition list to assign() with offset set to -1001 it will attempt to fetch committed offsets from the broker, and if that fails revert to auto.offset.reset.

If you want to manually specify the starting offset you can set the offset of the TopicPartition to an offset to your liking.

You can use `position()` to get the current consume position for the given partitions, and `committed()` to get the comitted offsets. Use `assignment()` to get the currently assigned partition set.

The produce() call is asynchronous, it will enqueue the message for transmission but the actual transmission to broker happens from a background thread.

You need to use flush() before exiting your program (or destroying the producer) to make sure outstanding messages are delivered.

For continually running producers you'll want to call poll() instead.

@stefanv The documentation for this function says it does total least-squares. It should minimize the sum of squares of the residuals in the input and output space. Each point-pair, as I understand it, contributes four residual values (two points, each with an x and y error). If we accept this, then there isn't any room to question the validity of the inputs, or question whether or not such a transformation exists.

The resulting transformation should be the one that minimizes sum of these residuals. It looks like this: https://gist.github.com/goretkin/c2705a98046e49d70dc3fe3a2688055f. I roughly followed the notation in [1].

It is perfectly well-defined to seek a resulting transformation that maps, in the least-error sense, two distinct inputs to the same output. If we consider only translations, I can ask "what is the best mapping that maps all these input points to the single output point `[0,0]`. The answer would be the translation corresponding to the set of the input points.

[1] http://people.csail.mit.edu/bkph/papers/Absolute_Orientation.pdf

We had a long discussion about this, as I recall.

Basically `ntiles_[x/y]` was a bad API choice from the start, for two huge reasons: it didn't follow our standard r/c conventions, and it left no easy way to generalize to higher dimensions. `kernel_size` solves both those problems. Frankly, I'd be just fine eliminating `ntiles_*` from the kwarg list and docstring entirely. Toss in a `**kwargs` for the next release, which will parse and output an informative error any time either one is found.

I'm in favor of making this change. We could add a check to see if `kernel_size` is an integer instead of a tuple, which would happen if someone used legacy code with positional arguments, and emit a `ValueError`. We really have to have a way forward to a cleaner API that doesn't involve messy, multi-year deprecations where we're teaching everyone to use it the wrong way. Breaking API for to expose a better API, when we can detect the old pattern(s) and warn or otherwise inform users how to fix their code, should be preferred or even encouraged. IMO. 

Not everyone on the core team agreed with me on this count, at least at the time.

After reading through the code some more, I think the difference is in the fact that the old approach masked the magnitude image, then applied a uniform filter and then subsampled the filtered magnitudes.

The new approach simply sums all magnitude values for coordinates that have the correct orientations in a small window (which I guess acts as a filter or subsampling strategy). However, it didn't correct the total. So if you divide total by (cell_rows * cell_columns), you get the following image:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/485186/11117153/adc03352-8937-11e5-8ebf-e9cb544cfd5e.png)

When I subtract the old result from this one, we get the following difference:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/485186/11117136/8f5524d6-8937-11e5-9416-38946d75b96e.png)

Which might still not be entirely what we want, but at least much closer to what we had before.

On a side-note I noticed that inside cell_hog we calculate 
```
   range_rows_stop = cell_rows/2
    range_rows_start = -range_rows_stop
    range_columns_stop = cell_columns/2
    range_columns_start = -range_columns_stop
```
Which are constant for all iterations, so we could move that calculation outside all the loops and just pass it along.

Continuing on the previous train of thought, `Task`s (and really the whole import/processing) process might be better as its own class, instead of mixed in with the `Catalog` base class, to clean things up and keep them better organized.  The import class would always be given the catalog, of course, and thus access to any required attributes/functions.  Likely, the same should happen with cleanup and sanitization: instead of just being a task used during import, this might be better as its own class, which one method of triggering its use, is via one of the import tasks.  Cleaning could also be merged with exporting/saving.

For debugging and data improvements, it would really help if both directions (import and cleaning/export) had a particular function that was run on each event, in addition to each task.  That way it would be easier to target particular events.  e.g.

Preserve a `Task` as simply a record of the task to be done, i.e. a simple wrapper for some json-data (including module, activity, etc).  For each task, subclass a new `Importer` class.

```
class Importer:
    def __init__(self, catalog, ...):
        # setup all of the variables currently in the `Task` objects

    def import_task(self, ...):
        # Load files, source information, etc; setup progress bar
        # ...
        for entry in entry_list:
            self.import_entry(entry, ...)

    def import_entry(self, ...):
        # Load/parse each entry, add to catalog
        # ...
```

Similar structure could be used for cleaning/exporting with an `def clean_task()` function and separate `def clean_entry()` function.

The reason I want the RealSense code in a new thread, is that the non-essential visualizations are being drawn from the main thread. In particular OpenCV imshow does not like to be in anything else than the main thread. In order to guarantee full fps processing of the images, the essential realsense camera algorithms are processed in a separate thread.

Please keep ROS related Issue on the [ROS realsense camera GitHub](https://github.com/intel-ros/realsense).

There should be ONE and only one version of librealsense install on your system. Librealsense does a check to ensure that any binary which uses the library was compiled with that exact version of the library.

If you are using ROS, you should stick with the ROS Debian packages as the realsense-camera Debian package is guaranteed to be compiled with the version of librealsense from the Debian package.

If for some reason you need to build the realsense-camera ROS package, it should be compiled against the Debian librealsense installed in /opt/ros/<distro>.  If you need to "hack on" librealsense and build it from code, then you have to uninstall both the realsense-camera and librealsense ROS Debian packages, build librealsense from source first, then build realsense-camera from source.

The librealsense team here is support the library cross-platform, and is not tracking any issues released to ROS. If you're still having ROS released issues, let's keep the tickets on the ROS GitHub. https://github.com/intel-ros/realsense

One more comment - when you actually get really close you RMS error will go to less than 1mm. However, by default the "depth unit" is 1=1000um (1mm). You can change that under the advanced mode to be, for example, 1=100um.  See the screenshot above. You now just need to make sure in software that you interpret your output depth as if each value is 100um, and not 1mm.  

There will be a limit to how close you can get with the D415 and D435. Basically the lenses are focused at hyperfocal 50cm, but have good focused images to closer to 20cm. Closer than this and the image will blur, causing a degradation of depth. Furthermore, when you get this close with such a large baseline (distance between imagers), stereo triangulation starts to break down - If you are looking at an object from opposite sides for the Left and Right imagers then it will of course not find a match.

```python
class RechargeSim(graphene.Mutation):
    class Arguments:
        msisdn = graphene.String(required=True)
        network_id = graphene.ID(required=True)
        product_id = graphene.ID(default_value='')
        airtime_amount = graphene.Float(default_value=0)

    recharge_id = graphene.Int()
    message = graphene.String()

    @staticmethod
    @graphql_secure
    def mutate(root, info, msisdn, network_id, product_id, airtime_amount):
        organisation = info.context.organisation

        if not organisation.allow_recharges_via_api:
            error = (
                'You do not have permission to perform recharges via the API,'
                ' please contact support to request access.'
            )
            raise Exception(error)

        if not organisation.recharge_callback_url:
            raise Exception('Please configure your Recharge Callback URL under Settings')

        # HACK: Optional fields do not seem to be working,
        # so we default to 0 in the field definition and then reset it here
        # Defaulting to None falls over.
        if airtime_amount == 0:
            airtime_amount = None

        _, network_id = from_global_id(network_id)
        if product_id:
            _, product_id = from_global_id(product_id)

        data = {
            'msisdn': msisdn,
            'network': network_id,
            'product': product_id,
            'airtime_amount': airtime_amount
        }

        form = AdhocRechargeForm(organisation, data)

        if not form.is_valid():
            raise Exception(form.errors.as_data())

        adhoc_recharge_id = form.recharge()

        notification = 'Request Queued. Status will be POSTed to your callback URL when ready'
        return RechargeSim(recharge_id=adhoc_recharge_id, message=notification)

```

The underlying ElementFinder (which almost all keywords are calling in one way or another) is, in 3.0 and also in earlier releases, calling Selenium `find_elements_*` methods, which will never raise an error from Selenium side (almost true, there are some corner cases when exception is raised from Selenium side). The `find_elements_*` methods are returning list of `WebElements` and if nothing is found, list is empty. Then the SL will raise its own exception based on keyword context and keyword logic. Therefore the SeleniumLibrary doesn't usually need logic to handle Selenium exceptions.

After discussion IRL with @kaplun:

- We support validation of ISBNs and ISSNs.
- We do not support normalisation of ISBNs/ISSNs.
- Normalisation of ISBNs are quite complex, so using external lib would be preferable.

*Proposed solution:*

- [ ] Add soft dependencies on python-stdnum or isbnlib. If the dependency is not installed, we implement a simple normalisation function which strips the entire ISBN.
- [ ] Implement ISSN validation

Here are links to the formal descriptions that I used to write the validators. URL resolvers were primarily based on information from http://identifiers.org/

I have merged the 1.0.1 changes and corrected the omission of a valid BioProject accession format.

- SRA https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK56913/#search.what_do_the_different_sra_accessi
- BioProject [DDBJ](https://www.ddbj.nig.ac.jp/bioproject/faq-e.html#project-accession) [ENA](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/submit/project-format) [NCBI](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/bioproject/docs/faq/#under-what-circumstances-is-it-n)
- BioSample [DDBJ](https://www.ddbj.nig.ac.jp/biosample/faq-e.html) [ENA](http://ena-docs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/prog_03.html#accession-numbers-in-the-receipt-xml) [NCBI](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/biosample/docs/submission/faq/)
- Ensembl https://www.ensembl.org/info/genome/stable_ids/prefixes.html
- UniProt https://www.uniprot.org/help/accession_numbers
- RefSeq https://academic.oup.com/nar/article-abstract/44/D1/D733/2502674 (Table 1)
- genome https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/browse/genome-assembly-database

NOTE: For the RefSeq validator, the pattern could/should be more strict in terms of the number of digits (6 or 9) following the various prefixes when that is specified by the standard, but it would make the regex quite a bit longer. The `NZ_` pattern is unusual and I couldn't find clear documentation for the format but `NZ_[A-Z]{4}` seems to be correct.

minCount is used to discard those words which appear less than minCount times in your training corpus.

The intuition behind this is that if your training corpus is large enough and if frequency of a particulat word is very less, it will act as a noise and the model will not converge properly. But, this is xorpus/case dependent and thus it is a hyper-parameter which you can tune to your need.

There are many good reasons why the config dict should not be serialized. 

1. That is a big-ass dict. Since in most cases it is repeating data, it is pretty bad to just blow up the json and storage just for these purposes. i.e., this is low information entropy. We should encode using the least bits.
2. Most things can be achieved via user_incar_settings, and that is definitely serialized. 
3. The whole idea of a InputSet is to standardize. If people modify the dict extensively, there really isn't a point to it is there? Generally if you are going to completely change the PSP set, you really should be writing a new InputSet (e.g., MP vs MIT relax sets). Note that the PSP has significant implications, not least in terms of compatibility. It is not a good idea to allow people to change the PSP and forget about it.

Like what @montoyjh said, you can add user_potcar_settings and user_kpoint_settings. Or perhaps even more generally, a user_config_dict_settings (though the usage is highly complex). But before I agree to such changes, I really need to understand (a) the use case, and (b) the consequences.

Finally, we actually had VaspInputSets that do not take structure in pmg < 4, and we explicitly refactored to one that did. The main reason here is that the actual structure has an effect on all inputs - INCAR, KPOINTS, POSCAR, POTCAR. There are many times you want the input set to perform some pre-processing of the structure (e.g., in the BS, you want to go to the standard cells). You can either say that the incar, kpoints, poscar, and potcar generation methods all need to call a "get_standardized_structure" before they generate the input, and this is repeating the same call in multiple locations, and some idiot coder can always forget to include such a call, resulting in hard to find bugs. Or you can simply ask that people supply a structure in the init and any standardization is done there and all the other methods simply use the *same* structure. Supply structure also allows people to supply history information, e.g., prev_incar etc.

Besides the inputs to the functions, I think another good requirement for "structure" featurizers should be that they return the same number of features regardless of the number of sites in the structure.

The featurizers that return a concatenated list of site features are problematic. For one, the number of features changes with the number of sites, which causes problems for many ML algorithms. Also, the order of inputs to the model depend on the order of the sites - meaning that reordering the sites could lead to different inputs/outputs for an ML model. Together, these factors make these features not easily "ML-ready."

Maybe we should refactor those "concatenating" structure featurizers into site featurizers by having them take the site index as input. Then, they could be turned into effective structure featurizers using the nice `SiteStatsFingerprint` adapter. 

Sound reasonable?

Aside: CoulombMatrix is a weird structure featurizer because it is often not used directly. At least in their 2015 paper, Faber *et al* advocate using the eigenvalues of the CM as inputs to a model and padding the eigenvalue list with zeros to make them all the same length. Even though the size of the feature does change with the number of sites, I'm OK with keeping it in the structure featurizer, though we should probably build the eigenvalue/padding opertaions into the featurizer.

Another simple alternative would be to provide this as an argument to the validation decorators, allowing full backwards compatibility. 
Yet another alternative is to call validates_schema methods when a field has already failed only if strict mode is off. As such when strict mode is on the errors are propagated immediately (or at least it appears so to the user), which follows the same pattern users would be familiar with for exceptions. This would also follow the principle of least astonishment - if an error is raised (as occurs when strict is True) then the user can expect that code further down the pipeline won't be called.

Ok, I just dived into the code and found out that it is very fishy. If you look at structure of `dump` method, it first calles pre_dump processors, then dump, then post_dump processors, then analysis of accumulated errors and running error handler. Thus error handler is never called if strict=True (except for pre_dump, which looks like a bug).

Although, dump and post_dump won't be called at all if previous stages have returned errors. And if those two stages return errors, exception will be immediately raised if strict=True. That means, that there is no point of analyzing strictness at last stage as you do, because the error was probably already raised. Except for pre_dump: this one does not raises exception. So, the proper way to fix it is to put `if self.strict: raise error` right after this line https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/schema.py#L483. No need to modify block where you are trying to fix it.

The whole implementation for error handler looks incorrect: it would be better to remove these lines:
https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/schema.py#L507-L508   and raise exception at the bottom of `dump` method.

As of removing deprecation, this should be done by a dedicated commit which removes it from everywhere, not just from a single place it is used. Also, that commit should only go to next major release since it breaks backward compatibility.

I stopped using contexts altogether, since they didn’t solve my problem. I wanted “dynamic” context that would change on per-load basis. If I remember correctly, the problem I encountered was that once a schema was created without a context, it would never get passed a context, even if it was called as a Nested schema from a parent schema with a context. Said a different way, the context is associated statically with a schema for the duration of its lifetime, rather than passed dynamically at load time. Sounds like you have the same but opposite problem: once a schema is created with a context, you can never get rid of it.

Not sure if anything has changed since I encountered this problem 2 years ago.

from marshmallow import fields, Schema, validates_schema, ValidationError





class ChildSchema(Schema):

    name = fields.Str()



    @validates_schema

    def validate_context(self, data):

        if self.context.get('foo') is not None:

            raise ValidationError('foo is set')



class ParentSchema(Schema):

    child = fields.Nested(ChildSchema())





class TestParentSchema():

    def test_with_context(self):

        schema_with_context = ParentSchema(context={'foo': 'bar'})



        result = schema_with_context.validate({'child': {'name': 'Joe'}})



        assert {'child': {'_schema': ['foo is set']}} == result



    def test_without_context(self):

        schema_without_context = ParentSchema()



        result = schema_without_context.validate({'child': {'name': 'Joe'}})



        assert {} == result

@gcbirzan we did not make this decision lightly.

We picked threads because there are exactly two concurrency primitives available in Python  *2 and 3*: threads and processes. Processes are useful for dealing with CPU-bound tasks, whereas threads are useful for dealing with I/O bound tasks. All of our clients are I/O bound. With the additional context the grpcio uses threads by default and that threads are the most compatible and portable way to achieve concurrency (both gevent and asyncio have ways to co-operate with threads), it makes the most sense for us to use threads.

We are aware and sensitive to the fact that users have some aversion to threads, especially with environments like uwsgi which disable threads by default. We try to limit our usage of threads in clients that are meant to be used in a web-application- pub/sub's publisher, for example, only uses one background thread (other than the grpc threads) at a time. The subscriber, on the other hand, has a much different use case and requires multiple threads to accomplish its behavior.

With all that said, I am open to discussing issues with this approach and possibly alternatives. We *need* concurrency in some of the clients, so it's unlikely we can remove threads altogether, however, it's certainly possible for us to make changes to support other concurrency strategies at certain points. For example, we intentionally designed the pub/sub subscriber so that a different type of `Pool` could be used to execute tasks.

I was able to get it working by building my own class

```python
from pynwb.spec import NWBNamespaceBuilder, NWBGroupSpec, NWBAttributeSpec
from pynwb import load_namespaces, NWBFile, NWBHDF5IO, get_class
from pynwb.file import MultiContainerInterface, register_class

ext_name = 'test_ext'
ns_path = ext_name + ".namespace.yaml"
ext_source = ext_name + ".extensions.yaml"

elem = NWBGroupSpec(neurodata_type_inc='NWBDataInterface',
                    neurodata_type_def='Elem',
                    quantity='+',
                    doc='doc',
                    attributes=[NWBAttributeSpec(name='data', doc='data',
                                                 dtype='double', required=True)])

grouper = NWBGroupSpec(neurodata_type_inc='NWBDataInterface',
                       neurodata_type_def='Grouper',
                       quantity='?',
                       doc='doc',
                       groups=[elem])

ns_builder = NWBNamespaceBuilder(ext_name + ' extensions', ext_name)

ns_builder.add_spec(ext_source, grouper)
ns_builder.export(ns_path)


load_namespaces(ns_path)

Elem = get_class('Elem', ext_name)


@register_class('Grouper', ext_name)
class Grouper(MultiContainerInterface):

    __clsconf__ = {
        'attr': 'elems',
        'type': Elem,
        'add': 'add_elem',
        'get': 'get_elem',
        'create': 'create_elem',
    }

    __help = 'test grouper'


elem1 = Elem(data=2.0, name='elem', source='source')
elem2 = Elem(data=3.0, name='elem2', source='source')

group = Grouper(name='group', source='source')
group.add_elem(elem1)
group.add_elem(elem2)

nwbfile = NWBFile("source", "a file with header data", "NB123A", '2018-06-01T00:00:00')
mod = nwbfile.create_processing_module(name='module', source='source',
                                       description='description')

mod.add_container(group)

with NWBHDF5IO('test_group', 'w') as io:
    io.write(nwbfile)
```

I think the best approach here would be to correct `get_class` so it generates the class correctly. I know you prefer custom classes but in cases where it's a straightforward constructor I think the auto-generated classes make sense because that means the nwb file can be entirely self-contained, and no external python libraries need to be maintained and distributed. If we can't support certain types of classes that's ok, but then I don't think we should be auto-generating classes that don't work. We should catch these cases and send a message to the user that they need to create a constructor themselves- at least until we get around to correcting the auto-generation feature for these types of classes.
